entry.title: Re: Screen Painter Blank in SAPMV45A screen
entry.content:
Good remark, but I don't know how "Reset SAP GUI settings and clear the SAP GUI cache" could solve this.

Instead, I guess that SAP GUI is corrupted and reinstallation could solve the bug.

@Tanuj_Patil: you may also switch to the "alphanumeric painter" via the ABAP Workbench settings from the menu, to verify quickly that SAPMV45A 0101 contains a layout. Or display the tab of elements to make sure they are present.

If the alphanumeric painter shows the screen layout, the installation of SAP GUI for Windows is corrupted, so reinstall the latest version.

entry.title: Introduction
entry.content:
Hi everyone!

I'm happy to join the SAP Community. I'm excited to learn, connect with fellow SAP professionals, and expand my knowledge. I look forward to participating in discussions, sharing ideas, and growing together with this amazing community.

Thank you, and I'm glad to be here!

entry.title: Bank Statement Processing – Documents Not Displayed in Posting Proposal for Uncleared Rows (CSV Impo
entry.content:
We are using the Bank Statement Processing functionality in SAP Business One to perform automatic bank reconciliation by importing bank statements in CSV format.

The bank statement is imported successfully without any errors. However, after the import, when we click Posting Proposal for Uncleared Rows, the corresponding SAP Business One documents (such as Incoming Payments, Outgoing Payments, or other relevant transactions) that should be available for reconciliation are not displayed.

As a result, we are unable to proceed with the automatic reconciliation process.

Could you please guide us on the following:

  • What are the prerequisites for documents to appear in the Posting Proposal window?
  • What matching criteria does SAP Business One use to identify and display documents for automatic reconciliation?
  • Are there any mandatory configuration settings or mapping requirements that need to be completed before using this functionality?
  • Are there any mandatory fields in the imported CSV file that are required for the posting proposal to identify the corresponding SAP Business One documents?

Please let us know if there are any additional settings or checks that we should perform to resolve this issue.

entry.title: Wrong value in CDS View YY1_SUPDEMANDITEMS
entry.content:
I am facing a mismatch between the output of custom CDS View YY1_SUPDEMANDITEMS and T- code: MD04. 
My question is how data is loaded from T code: MD04 to cds: YY1_SUPDEMANDITEMS.

S4:   
T code: MD04  
For Material:- BSA1007208401, Plant:-4060
Here, Requiremnt:-1281  

   
S4 CdS:- YY1_SUPDEMANDITEMS 
For Material:- BSA1007208401, Plant:-4060 
mrpelement_open_quantity :-1460  

 

Sum of mrpelemnt_open_qnantity = 1460 , which is not matching with T code value.

entry.title: What Disappears in 2H 2026: SAP SuccessFactors Basic Auth & Direct Corporate IdP Integration
entry.content:
The single sentence

With the 2H 2026 release, SAP deletes three login-side capabilities from SAP SuccessFactors — UI Basic Authentication, third-party corporate IdP direct integration, and Partial Organization SSO — plus support for multiple asserting parties wired directly to SuccessFactors (KBA 3472405) [1][2][3][4]. After that, there is no direct authentication into SAP SuccessFactors — every login must flow through SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service (IAS), either as the main IdP or as a proxy in front of your corporate IdP [1][12].

 

The Preview-first trap most plans miss

Preview data centers lose Basic Authentication and direct-IdP login on October 12, 2026, roughly a month before Production [12]. Preview/Test is normally where you rehearse the migration. So:

If you haven't migrated your Preview tenant to IAS by mid-October 2026, you can lose basic-auth access to your own test environment before you've finished testing the migration you were going to run in Production.

The practical sequencing consequence: treat Preview as its own deadline (October 12), not as a warm-up you can leave until October. Migrate and validate Preview well before mid-October, so Preview is already on IAS and available to test against while you prepare Production for November 13.

 

What is deleted with the 2H 2026 release

Four distinct things, all under this one release. Be precise about each: they fail differently and you check for them differently.

1. Basic Authentication for the UI

End users logging into SuccessFactors with a username and password held in SuccessFactors itself, the "company ID + username + password" login screen. This is the classic native login.

What breaks: anyone who authenticates this way can no longer log in. They must instead authenticate through IAS (either against the IAS password store, or forwarded by IAS to your corporate IdP) [1]. Note that two populations are already IAS-only ahead of everyone else: new hires (onboardees) and external learners can only log in through IAS [5]. So if onboarding or external learning is live, part of this is effectively already in force for you.

2. Third-party corporate IdP direct integration

Your corporate identity provider (Entra ID / Azure AD, Okta, Ping, OneLogin, Google Workspace) configured as a SAML asserting party directly against SuccessFactors, with no SAP identity layer in between.

The word doing all the work is direct. You are not losing your corporate IdP. You are losing the ability to point it straight at SuccessFactors "without SAP Identity Services as proxy" [1]. The trust that currently terminates at SF must instead terminate at IAS, which then fronts SF. Your users keep authenticating against Entra/Okta/Ping exactly as before: the direct SF-to-IdP trust is what's removed [1][2].

What breaks: the direct SAML trust stops working, and users routed through it can't authenticate, unless that same IdP has been re-established as a corporate IdP inside IAS first.

3. Support for multiple asserting parties (direct)

A specific case of #2 worth calling out because larger orgs hit it: if you currently run more than one corporate IdP as multiple SAML asserting parties directly against SuccessFactors (e.g., different regions or business units, with a chooser screen or default asserting party), support for multiple asserting parties is deprecated by this date [4]. The replacement is to configure those same multiple IdPs as multiple corporate identity providers within IAS, with IAS doing the routing [4].

What breaks: the multi-IdP selection at the SF layer goes away; if you haven't rebuilt that routing in IAS, users behind the secondary asserting parties lose their path in.

4. Partial Organization SSO

The side-by-side pattern where one group of users authenticates through a corporate IdP while another group still logs in with a SuccessFactors username and password. Partial Organization SSO reached end of maintenance May 16, 2025 and is deleted with the 2H 2026 release [3].

What breaks: the side-by-side mechanism at the SF layer is removed. The capability itself, routing different populations to different authenticators, is rebuilt inside IAS as conditional authentication (KBA 2954556) [3][13]. So this isn't "you lose partial SSO forever," it's "partial SSO moves into IAS and you have to configure it there."

What "deleted" concretely means

Not "deprecated but tolerated." Deleted. SAP's language is unambiguous: after the deprecation completes, customers can no longer access SAP SuccessFactors with basic authentication or third-party corporate IdP direct integration [1][12]. In practice:

  • Basic-auth users and direct-IdP users who have not moved to IAS cannot authenticate: this is a login outage for those populations, not a warning banner [1].
  • New hires (onboardees) and external learners are already IAS-only, so unmigrated onboarding is the population that breaks first, not last [5].
  • IAS itself remains the answer for everyone: password users authenticate against IAS, and corporate-IdP users are forwarded by IAS to their IdP [4]. Everything ends up behind IAS.

Why SAP is doing this

Worth a paragraph, because it frames the migration as more than compliance homework [12]:

  • Basic authentication is simply weaker. It offers less security than the methods common to modern cloud applications: there's no additional mechanism to verify the user, and passwords are routinely reused across accounts and apps, which raises the risk of compromise. Moving to IAS is what unlocks multi-factor and risk-based authentication for SuccessFactors [12].
  • One identity platform instead of many point integrations. Putting IAS in front (as main or proxy IdP) unifies the login experience across all SAP applications and lets SAP harmonize UI and API authentication on the same platform, which in turn makes authorization management cleaner. It also cuts maintenance, because IAS ships pre-built integration and provisioning to SuccessFactors and other SAP products, rather than you hand-maintaining a direct IdP-to-SF trust [12].

And the flip side, if you don't migrate [12]:

  1. You can't adopt current security and compliance baselines such as MFA for cloud applications.
  2. You're left on an old, unsupported authentication architecture that is itself slated for deprecation.
  3. After the deprecation completes, you lose access via basic auth or direct-IdP entirely.

What is NOT going away with the 2H 2026 release (the confusion-killers)

This is where most planning goes wrong. Several things sound like they belong to this release and don't. Pin these down so nobody on your team conflates them.

 

API Basic Authentication (the OData/SOAP auth method)November 12, 2027 (phased)A separate deprecation on the API side. End of maintenance was June 2, 2023; deletion is November 12, 2027, with Basic Auth off by default in 1H 2027 (opt-in for migration) and fully disabled in 2H 2027. Don't let this later date lull you: login is November 13, 2026, and the EC items below die even earlier [6].
Login using SuccessFactors employment usernameMay 15, 2026 (earlier)A narrower login variant, deprecated ahead of the main date. After it, users can still log in via IAS (login name + password) or via basic auth using account login name until the login deprecation; employment-username logins must switch to account login name [5].
SOAP-based Employee Central SFAPIs (except CompoundEmployee), plus Basic Auth for EC integration connectivity (core hybrid / payroll, ref. ECT-263763)November 20, 2026API/integration items that die a year before general API Basic Auth (Nov 2027). If you run SOAP SFAPIs or core-hybrid/payroll integrations, this earlier date governs them, not the 2027 one [8].
SSO signing certificateAlready expired June 2, 2025Not a future date at all: it's already gone. If you're still direct, this already bit you [9].
Report Center SFTP report deliveryNot affectedUses the platform's internal scheduling framework, authenticated separately. No change required, a common false alarm (KBA 3724884) [7].

The one-line takeaway: login and API are separate tracks with different dates. Login/UI Basic Auth lands with the 2H 2026 release (Preview October 12, Production November 13, 2026). General API Basic Auth runs to November 12, 2027 (off by default in 1H 2027, fully disabled in 2H 2027); but SOAP SFAPIs and EC integration connectivity die a year earlier, on November 20, 2026, so 2027 is not blanket breathing room [1][6][8][12].

How to tell if you're exposed

Concrete self-checks, not a project: do these first.

  • Basic Auth (UI): if the SuccessFactors default username/password login page appears and a login succeeds, you have basic-auth users. If users land on the IAS login page or are redirected to their corporate IdP, they're already behind IAS [3].
  • Direct corporate IdP: in Provisioning under Single Sign-On (SSO) Settings, check your SAML asserting parties. A corporate IdP configured as an enabled asserting party directly (with IAS not the enabled asserting party) is a direct integration that must move [4].
  • Multiple asserting parties: more than one enabled asserting party (or a user-facing "choose your IdP" screen) means you're relying on the multi-asserting-party support that's being removed [4].
  • Partial Organization SSO: same test as Basic Auth: if some users hit the default SF username/password page and log in successfully while others are redirected, Partial Org SSO is still enabled and you're exposed [3].

If every population already lands on IAS or a corporate IdP (never the native SF login page), you may be further along than you think: IAS has been auto-deployed for tenants created after December 9, 2022 (KBA 2791410) [12].

What to do about it (the short version)

The fix for all four items is the same destination: move authentication onto IAS. The process runs through the Upgrade Center: run "Initiate the SAP Cloud Identity Services Identity Authentication Service Integration," configure IAS/IPS and your corporate-IdP trust (and rebuild multi-IdP routing and conditional/partial authentication inside IAS via KBA 2954556), then run "Activate SuccessFactors Identity Authentication Service Integration" (KBA 2791410) [12][13]. Activation is a one-way door and forces a successful test first, so do it in Preview before Production [12].

The reason to start now, not in Q4: SAP is running the Initiate (and then Activate) tasks for tenants not yet on IAS, on a mass-enablement schedule between July and September 2026, in support of the 2H 2026 release [11]. That keeps your users' access continuous, but an automated Activate is an uncontrolled cutover: no testing window you designed, no conditional-auth rules you built, no dry run. Running Initiate yourself, ahead of the automation, is how you keep control of the configuration and give yourself runway to validate before the release [11]. IAS adoption is mandatory by November 2026 regardless (KBA 3097769) [10].

Layer the Preview-first trap on top of this: with Preview deletion on October 12, your practical window to have Preview migrated and testable is even shorter than the November headline suggests.

Resources & getting help

SAP has put real enablement behind this migration. Use it [12]:

  • Announcement blog: Deprecation of Basic Auth / 3rd Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SuccessFactors.
  • Migration community blog: Migration to SAP Cloud Identity Authentication (IAS/IPS), with resources and how-to videos.
  • Mass-enablement blog: Mass enablement of Initiate IAS Upgrade and Activate IAS tasks to enforce…, the authoritative source for the July–September 2026 automation [11].
  • Help Portal: Migration to SAP Cloud Identity Services – Identity Authentication Service, and the release-note page for this deprecation [2].
  • Step-by-Step Guide of SF to IAS Integration Process and Common Troubleshooting Tips: SAP Community (search the title; a practical walkthrough with troubleshooting).
  • SAP Discovery Center: Mission: Learn to integrate HCM with SAP Cloud Identity Services in SAP SuccessFactors and migrate to IAS/IPS (SAP has moved the IAS/IPS e-learning here).
  • IAS/IPS Open Office Hours: now held once monthly via the SAP Learning Hub (a valid S-user and one-time Enterprise Support Academy sign-up are required); registration is linked from the migration community blog above.
  • If something breaks during migration, open an incident under component LOD-SF-PLT-IAS (Identity Authentication Services with BizX) [1][12].
  • For onboarding-specific IAS issues, use LOD-SF-OBX-IAS;
  • For IAS product issues, BC-IAM-IDS;
  • For IPS configuration, BC-IAM-IPS.

References

Numbered in order of first appearance. Every SAP KBA below is hyperlinked to its public SAP support page. Verify live dates on the SAP Help Portal before finalizing any plan. SAP has revised adjacent deadlines before, and the two-wave release timing (Preview October 12 / Production November 13, 2026) is exactly the kind of thing worth re-checking against the live page.

[1] SAP KBA 3472405: Deprecation of Basic Authentication and Third-Party Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite. End of maintenance June 2, 2025; deletion with the 2H 2026 release; "no direct integration to SAP SuccessFactors after deprecation." Component LOD-SF-PLT-IAS.

[2] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Basic Authentication and Third-Party Corporate Identity Provider (IdP) Direct Integration with SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite (What's New / release information page).

[3] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Partial Organization SSO in SAP SuccessFactors HCM suite. End of maintenance May 16, 2025; deleted November 13, 2026 (body text contains a stray "November 15, 2026"); includes the default-login-page detection test.

[4] SAP: Setting Up SAP SuccessFactors with Identity Authentication (integration admin guide). Source for IAS as real vs proxy IdP, asserting-party selection behavior, and "support for multiple asserting parties will also be deprecated by November 2026."

[5] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of User Login Using Employment Username. Employment-username login deprecated by May 15, 2026; basic auth deprecated by November 13, 2026; new hires (onboardees) and external learners are IAS-only.

[6] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Basic Authentication for APIs (the API side; deletion revised to November 12, 2027). See the API companion guide.

[7] SAP KBA 3724884: Potential impacts of Basic Authentication Deprecation for Report Center SFTP schedules (SFTP report delivery not affected).

[8] SAP Help Portal: Deprecation of Employee Central SFAPIs Except CompoundEmployee (deletion November 20, 2026 as publicly confirmed).

[9] SAP Help Portal: Renewal of SAP SuccessFactors HCM Suite Single Sign-On Certificate (certificate expired June 2, 2025).

[10] SAP KBA 3097769: IAS / IPS – Is IAS implementation mandatory for all SuccessFactors customers? (mandatory by November 2026).

[11] SAP Community (Product and Customer Updates): Mass enablement of Initiate IAS Upgrade and Activate IAS tasks to enforce…, plus SAP's direct customer notice. Source for the July–September 2026 mass-enablement window and the automatic Initiate/Activate behavior.

[12] SAP KBA 2791410: Integrating SuccessFactors with Identity Authentication (IAS) through the Upgrade Center, together with SAP's customer communication for this deprecation. Source for the Initiate → configure → Activate process, IAS auto-deployment for tenants after December 9, 2022, the two-wave deletion dates (Preview October 12 / Production November 13, 2026), SAP's rationale, and the resource list.

[13] SAP KBA 2954556: How to implement Partial SSO after Identity Authentication (IAS) upgrade on SuccessFactors (the conditional-authentication replacement for Partial Organization SSO).

entry.title: Re: Bank Statement Processing – Documents Not Displayed i...
entry.content:
I think you might be using BSP the wrong way. When you import bank statement, the idea is that you have open invoices for which you will generate payments and reconcile them.

So when you click on Posting Proposal, the system should match bank statement line to some open invoice. After that, you Finalize the bank statement, which will generate payment document with postings and reconciliation between invoice and payment.

So now you need to set bank statement matching criteria so that Posting Proposal can match statement line with open invoice (by using payment reference most commonly).

entry.title: Gerüchteküche - erste eigene Funktionen: Einheit 2 Lektion 2
entry.content:
An English version of this lesson is available at: Get Coding with Snap! - Building Up to AI

 

Videolektion "Gerüchteküche"

In dieser Lektion beschäftigst du dich mit Funktionen - Programmbausteine, die einen Rückgabewert haben. Während du wilde Gerüchte verbreitest, lernst du deine ersten eigenen Funktionen zu bauen. Zudem erfährst du mehr über Listen, eine Datenstruktur, die mehrere Elemente umfassen kann. 

Folge den Schritten aus dem Video in Snap!. Die Aufgaben bauen teilweise auf das Projekt aus dem Video auf. 

 

 

 

Du bist dran

  • Erstelle einen weiteren Reporter (Funktion), der deine Sätze noch “gerüchtiger” macht, und füge diesen Block ebenfalls in deinen gossip Reporter ein. Hier sind einige mögliche Beispiele: 
    • “Hey, I’ve got news! “
    • “Did you know: “
    • “I’ve heard a crazy story. “

 

  • Schreibe ein “Wahrsage”-Programm. Lasse Benutzer:innen eine Frage stellen, die dein Programm beantwortet, indem zufällig aus einer Auswahl von Antworten ausgewählt wird. 
    Wahrsager:innen geben meist keine klaren und direkten Antworten - füge deshalb vor allem vage Aussagen wie “Dir steht eine aufregende Zukunft bevor” in deine Auswahl an Antworten ein.

    Um Fragen an eine Benutzer:in zu stellen, kannst du diesen Block verwenden:

    Die Antwort ist in diesem Block gespeichert:

     

  • Hier findest du die Aufgaben auch zum Herunterladen:

    • als PDF:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/PdcAdpy?rc=10&doi=SAP1326699 
    • als editierbare docx Version:  https://dam.sap.com/mac/u/a/sibDEgo?rc=10&doi=SAP1326700 

    Lösungsvorschläge für Einheit 2 - Lektion 1 findest du in dieser Collection .

 

 

Zusammenfassung - das hast du diese Lektion gelernt

Bausteine dieser Lektion: 

Erzeugt eine Liste, in der Zahlen, Text oder auch weitere Listen, Blöcke oder Objekte abgelegt werden können. Die Anzahl der Elemente in der Liste kann durch Klicken auf die Pfeilspitzen an der rechten Seite erweitert oder verringert werden.  
Berichtet ein bestimmtes Element aus der Liste, die sich im rechten Eingabefeld befindet. Das ausgegebene Element basiert auf der Eingabe im ersten Eingabefeld. Dort können Zahlen verwendet oder spezielle Eingaben wie "zufällig" aus dem Dropdown-Menü ausgewählt werden.  
Verbindet Buchstaben oder Wörter zu einem längeren Text.
Achte darauf, dass du die Leerzeichen nicht vergisst, wenn du mehrere Wörter zu einem Satz verbinden möchtest. Durch Klicken auf die Pfeilspitzen kannst du die Anzahl der Eingaben erweitern oder verringern. 

Notwendig in benutzerdefinierten Berichtsblöcken (auch Reporter oder Funktionen genannt). Berichtet den Wert im Eingabefeld in einer kleinen Sprechblase am Block. 

Verwendet in der Blockdefinition eines Berichtsblocks, berichtet dieser Berichtsblock den Wert im Eingabefeld des berichte Blocks. 

Weitere Blöcke, die sich in der Definition nach diesem Block befinden, werden nicht evaluiert. 

Kann mit dem Modul "Spracherkennung und Sprachausgabe" importiert werden. 
Benutzt die eingebaute Text-to-Speech Funktion des Browsers, um den Text im ersten Eingabefeld laut ausgeben zu lassen. Mit den anderen Eingaben können Akzent, Tonhöhe und Geschwindigkeit der Ausgabe angepasst werden. 

 

Listen

In dieser Lektion hast du dich mit Listen beschäftigt.

Listen sind sogenannte zusammengesetzte Datentypen. Das bedeutet, dass sie mehrere Werte zu einer einzigen Einheit zusammenfassen. Diese Werte können Zahlen, Texte, andere Listen oder sogar Objekte bzw. Blöcke sein.
Du hast bereits in der letzten Lektion eine Liste von Objekten verwendet, als du ein zufälliges Element aus Attribut andere Objekte ausgewählt hast – also ein zufälliges Objekt aus einer Liste der anderen Objekte im Projekt.

Listen sind geordnet. Das bedeutet, dass die Reihenfolge der Elemente erhalten bleibt und jedes Element einer Liste einen Index besitzt, über den darauf zugegriffen werden kann. Du kannst deshalb den Element  _ von _ Block benutzen, um auf bestimmte Elemente zuzugreifen. 

Die Werte in Listen müssen nicht eindeutig sein, derselbe Wert kann mehrfach in einer Liste vorkommen.

Neben Blöcken mit denen du auf Werte in einer Liste zugreifen kannst, gibt es auch Blöcke um neue Werte hinzufügen oder vorhandene Werte löschen. Alle roten Listenblöcke findest du in der Kategorie „Variablen“.

Die roten Listenblöcke findest du in der Kategorie „Variablen“. Listen werden mit dem Block Liste erstellt. Durch Klicken auf den nach rechts zeigenden Pfeil kannst du der Liste ein neues Element hinzufügen. Mit Umschalt-Klick auf den Pfeil werden gleich drei neue Elemente hinzugefügt. Ein Klick auf den nach links zeigenden Pfeil entfernt Elemente wieder. Listen können verschiedene Arten von Werten enthalten, zum Beispiel Zahlen, Texte, andere Listen, Objekte oder Blöcke.

 

Funktionen

In dieser Lektion hast du außerdem gelernt, wie du eine eigene Funktion (auch Reporter- oder Berichtsblock genannt) erstellen kannst – einen ovalen Block, der einen Wert zurückgibt.

In der Mathematik ist eine Funktion eine Vorschrift, die 0 oder mehr Eingaben entgegennimmt und daraus genau eine Ausgabe erzeugt. Dieselbe Eingabe liefert dabei immer dieselbe Ausgabe.

In der Informatik wird der Begriff Funktion etwas allgemeiner verwendet. Eine Funktion ist ein Teil einer Programmiersprache (in Snap! ein Block), der eine bestimmte Aufgabe ausführt und einen Wert zurückgibt. Funktionen sollten wenn möglich keinen Zustand speichern und keine Seiteneffekte haben.

Wenn du mehr über Funktionen erfahren möchtest, schau dir diese Vorlesung  (Englisch) aus „the Beauty and Joy of Computing“ von Dan Garcia an.

Um eine eigene Funktion zu erstellen, wählst du im Dialog „Neuer Block“ als Blocktyp „Funktion“ aus. Der „Block-Editor“ fügt der Blockdefinition dann automatisch einen berichte Block hinzu. Der Wert, den du in den Eingabeschlitz des berichte-Blocks einsetzt, wird beim Auswerten des Blocks in einer Sprechblase angezeigt.

Um eine benutzerdefinierte Funktion zu erstellen, wähle “Funktion” als Blockform aus. Dann füge das, was der Block berichten soll, in der Blockdefinition in das Eingabefeld des berichte-Blocks ein.

 

Module mit zusätzlichen Blöcken laden

Außerdem hast du gelernt, wie du thematische Module mit zusätzlichen Blöcken importieren kannst.

Gehe zum Datei-Menü und wähle „Module…“, um ein Dialogfenster mit allen Block-Erweiterungen zu öffnen. Wähle das gewünschte Modul aus und klicke auf „Importieren“, um die Blöcke des entsprechenden Moduls am Ende der Blockpalette hinzuzufügen.

Im oberen rechten Bereich des Dialogfensters befindet sich eine Vorschau, die alle Blöcke des ausgewählten Moduls anzeigt.

Klicke auf Datei-Menü – “Module…”, um ein Dialogfenster mit allen vorhandenen Modulen zu öffnen. Verwende die Suche, um auf ein bestimmtes Modul zuzugreifen oder scrolle durch alle Module. Du kannst die Blöcke des ausgewählten Moduls in einem Vorschaufenster auf der rechten Seite sehen. Klickst du auf “Importieren”, werden die Blöcke in dein Projekt geladen. Importierte Blöcke erscheinen wie benutzerdefinierte Blöcke am Ende der jeweiligen Kategorie.

 

 

Und jetzt?

Das wars mit Einheit 2 - Lektion 2: Gerüchteküche - erste eigene Funktionen.

  • Zur nächsten Lektion des Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/klonen-aus-eins-mach-viele-einheit-2-lektion-3/ba-p/14442210 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite von Einheit 2 geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/einheit-2-bausteine-f%C3%BCr-syst... 
  • Zur Übersichtsseite des gesamten Kurses geht es hier: https://community.sap.com/t5/sap-young-thinkers-program-blog-posts/programmieren-mit-snap-building-u... 
  • Eine Englische Version des Kurses, bei der ein Zertifikat erworben werden kann, findet sich hier: https://learning.sap.com/courses/get-coding-with-snap-building-up-to-ai 

 

Dieses Material ist veröffentlicht unter der CC 4.0 Lizenz durch das Snap! Team bei SAP

entry.title: How to implement Finance on a UPA System - The Training Portfolio at a Glance!
entry.content:
Ready to build practical SAP S/4HANA finance skills for a world of parallel accounting?

Universal Parallel Accounting (UPA) is becoming an important foundation for finance implementations in SAP S/4HANA. If you work with financial accounting, asset accounting, or end-to-end record-to-report processes, now is a great time to strengthen your knowledge and get hands-on with learning content built on a UPA-enabled training system.

Universal Parallel Accounting (UPA) helps organizations prepare and present financial statements according to different accounting standards, such as group GAAP and local GAAP, by using ledgers across end-to-end processes. This supports consistent parallel value flows throughout the value chain — from inventory and production to overhead and asset accounting. With UPA, organizations benefit from full audit trails, greater automation and accuracy for parallel legal valuations, and simplified configuration compared with traditional parallel accounting approaches.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, UPA is activated by default. For SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and SAP S/4HANA, it must be activated — ideally at the beginning of a greenfield implementation.

To help learners prepare for this shift, we have been expanding our SAP Learning portfolio with courses that run on a UPA-enabled training system.

The journey started with the launch of the learning journey Implementing Record-to-Report in SAP S/4HANA, which helps learners build practical implementation skills for core record-to-report processes.

We have now added two more courses to support learners who want to deepen their expertise in accounts payable, accounts receivable, and asset accounting:

  • Managing Customer and Vendor Accounts
  • Configuring Basic Settings for Asset Accounting

Explore the new courses today and continue building the skills you need for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition finance implementations. More courses are already in preparation, so stay tuned as the portfolio continues to grow over the coming months.

Best regards,
Mónika

entry.title: Re: Issue with Multiple Forms Under a Single Output Type
entry.content:
Hello @kkumara A single Output Type cannot generate multiple Form Templates simultaneously through Output Parameter Determination.

The Output Parameter Determination framework determines only one form template per output item. If multiple determination records exist for the same output type, SAP evaluates them according to the determination rules (sequence, conditions, receiver role, etc.) and selects the first matching record, rather than generating multiple forms.

BR
Rajat

entry.title: Data Migration Strategy: Moving Employee Data from SAP S/4HANA/SAP ERP to SAP SuccessFactors EC
entry.content:
If you've been part of an Employee Central implementation, you already know the pattern: the module configuration goes smoothly, the business process design workshops wrap up on schedule, and then — data migration quietly becomes the workstream that determines whether go-live is smooth or painful.

Moving employee data from SAP S/4HANA (or SAP ERP/ECC) into SuccessFactors Employee Central (EC) isn't just a technical export/import exercise. You're moving data from an on-premise HCM model — Personnel Administration and Organizational Management — into a cloud-based, MDF (Metadata Framework) driven data model. The structures don't map 1:1, and treating this as "just another data load" is where most timelines start slipping.

Here's the strategy I use to structure this workstream, from scoping through cutover.

Start With a Real Data Object Inventory

Before touching extraction, get explicit about what's actually moving. I split scope into two buckets:

Foundation / Organizational data (MDF objects)

  • Legal Entity, Business Unit, Division, Department, Cost Center
  • Payscale Area, Payscale Type, Payscale Group, Payscale Level
  • Location, Job Classification (Job Code), Pay Grade, Pay Range
  • Position (if Position Management is in scope)

Person / Employment data

  • Personal Info, Biographical Info, National ID, Address, Email, Phone
  • Employment Details, Job Info, Compensation Info
  • Job Relationships (manager, HR business partner)
  • Global Assignment info, where relevant
  • Employment history — and how much of it, which is a design decision in its own right (more below)

The mistake I see teams make repeatedly: trying to carry SAP PA/OM structures across as-is. Infotypes like 0000, 0001, 0002, 0006, 0007, 0008, 0105, and 0041, along with OM objects 1000, 1001, and 1002, need to be explicitly mapped to EC's Foundation Objects and the EC data model — not just field-matched. The underlying logic is different enough that skipping this mapping exercise shows up later as broken assignments and bad reconciliation numbers.

Category

SAP Source (PA/OM)

SuccessFactors EC Target

Org structure

Org Units, Cost Centers (OM: 1000, 1001, 1002)

Legal Entity, BU, Division, Department, Cost Center (Foundation Objects/MDF)

Job & pay structure

Positions, Jobs, Pay Scale (IT1013, IT0008)

Position (MDF), Job Classification, Pay Grade, Pay Range

Personal data

IT0002, IT0006, IT0105

Personal Info, Biographical Info, National ID, Address, Email/Phone

Employment data

IT0000, IT0001, IT0041

Employment Details, Job Info, Job Relationships

Compensation

IT0008, IT0014, IT0015

Compensation Info, Recurring/Non-Recurring Pay Components

Global assignment

IT0710, OM relationships

Global Assignment Info (concurrent/relocated employment)

Time off balances

IT2006, cluster tables

Time Off — Time Account balances (if in phase 1 scope)

Choosing Your Migration Approach

There isn't a single "right" tool — it comes down to data volume, complexity, and how much cleansing your source data actually needs.

  • SAP-provided EC tools: Import & Export in Admin Center using CSV/XLSX templates generated straight from the EC data model, or the Employee Central Migration Cockpit for mapping-driven, historically complex loads.
  • Infoporter: SAP's standard tool purpose-built for migrating data from on-premise SAP HCM to SuccessFactors using a mapping-driven approach — worth evaluating early since it's designed specifically for this ECC/S4-to-EC scenario rather than being a generic loader.
  • Integration Center: better suited to smaller volumes or ongoing delta loads post-go-live than to the initial bulk migration.
  • Third-party ETL (SAP Data Services, Boomi, custom scripts): comes into play when the source data needs heavier transformation before it's fit to load.

In practice, most large migrations end up hybrid — extraction and transformation handled by scripts or SAP Data Services, with Infoporter, the Migration Cockpit, or EC import templates handling the actual load.

The Phased Approach

I break this into eight phases. Foundation objects always load before employee data — job and org assignments in EC reference Foundation Object IDs that need to already exist, so getting this sequence wrong is the single most common cause of failed mock loads.

Phase

Activities

Key Deliverable

Typical Duration

1. Scoping

Data object inventory, ID strategy, history depth decision, Position vs. Job-based design

Migration scope & design document

2–3 weeks

2. Extraction

ABAP/report-based extracts from PA/OM, source data profiling

Raw legacy data extract

1–2 weeks

3. Cleansing

Dedup, mandatory field validation, orphaned assignment fixes

Clean staging dataset

3–5 weeks

4. Mapping

Infotype-to-EC field mapping, picklist mapping, MDF object mapping

Mapping specification (signed off)

2–3 weeks

5. Foundation load

Legal Entity, Location, Department, Cost Center, Job Classification, Position

Populated Foundation Objects

1 week/cycle

6. Employee load

Biographical → Employment → Job → Compensation → Job Relationships, in sequence

Populated EC employee records

1–2 weeks/cycle

7. Mock loads

3–4 cycles into test tenant, reconciled after each

Reconciliation reports

4–6 weeks total

8. Cutover

Final delta load, data freeze on SAP HCM, go-live validation

Production-ready EC tenant

1 week

Design Decisions You Can't Defer

A few decisions shape the entire migration and are genuinely hard to walk back once mock loads are underway:

  1. How much history to migrate. This is rarely a one-size-fits-all answer — I've seen three approaches work depending on the customer:
  • Current state plus one year of compensation history, to keep scope manageable
  • Only the hire record and the "top of stack" record carried over from ECC
  • Treating the top-of-stack record itself as the hire record in EC

Which one fits depends heavily on the legacy system's design and any third-party integrations sitting downstream of EC — there's no default answer here, so this needs to be worked through with the customer rather than assumed.

  1. Employee ID strategy. Reuse the SAP Personnel Number as the EC User ID/Person ID, or generate new IDs? This ripples into every downstream integration — payroll replication, SSO — so it needs to be locked early, not discovered mid-build.
  2. Position vs. Job-based management in EC. This is a structural decision about how the target system will operate, not a mapping exercise you can leave to the ETL team.
  3. Concurrent / Global assignments. SAP OM structures don't translate cleanly here and need explicit target-state design rather than a default mapping.

RACI: Who Owns What

A sample RACI for a typical SI-led engagement — adjust to your actual governance structure:

Activity

Client HR

SI Functional

SI Technical/ETL

Client IT/Basis

Data object inventory & scope

A

R

C

I

Legacy data extraction

C

I

R

A

Data cleansing & validation

R

A

C

I

Field & picklist mapping

C

A/R

C

I

Foundation object load

I

R

A

I

Employee data load

I

A/R

R

I

Mock load reconciliation

R

A

C

I

Cutover execution

A

R

R

C

R = Responsible, A = Accountable, C = Consulted, I = Informed

Reconciliation Isn't Optional

After every mock load — not just before go-live — compare record counts and key field values (headcount by legal entity, active/inactive status, compensation figures) between SAP source and EC target. A few practices that pay off:

  • Run 3–4 mock load cycles into a test EC tenant before cutover
  • Reconcile right after each cycle, not just at the end
  • Track discrepancies in a defect log with clear, named ownership

Automate this. Manual spot-checks don't scale once you're past a few hundred employees, and they miss the systemic errors — like a picklist mapping gap — that show up consistently across records rather than in one-off cases.

Pitfalls Worth Flagging Early

Pitfall

Impact

Mitigation

Foundation object load sequence errors

Employee loads fail (e.g., Department references a missing Cost Center)

Enforce strict load order; validate the dependency chain before each cycle

Effective-dating mismatches

Incorrect history or job info gaps in EC

Map SAP infotype date logic explicitly to EC's effective-dated model early

Underestimated cleansing effort

Schedule slippage, repeated mock load failures

Budget 40–60% of total migration effort for cleansing; start early

Picklist mapping gaps

Load errors or blank/defaulted values in EC

Complete the picklist mapping workbook before the first mock load

Payroll replication misalignment

Payroll parallel run disrupted at cutover

Align the migration cutover date with the payroll parallel run calendar

Closing Thoughts

The tool you pick to load data — Infoporter, the Migration Cockpit, or a custom ETL pipeline — matters less than the discipline you bring to scoping, mapping, and reconciliation. Foundation data design, the history-depth decision, and cutover timing deserve to be treated as first-class design conversations with the customer, not technical details to sort out later in the build. That's usually what separates a clean go-live from a few extra months of stabilization.

Would be curious to hear how others have approached the history-migration question in particular — "top of stack as hire record" vs. carrying forward real history tends to be one of the more debated calls on these projects.

Feel free to drop questions or your own lessons learned in the comments.

entry.title: One Blog, Two Strategic Topics: API-Centric Integration and MCP Gateway
entry.content:
This blog brings together to important concepts shaping the future of enterprise integration: API-centric integration and the MCP Gateway.

To start, the following resources provide a clear introduction to both topics and the outcomes we want to achieve:

  • API-Centric Integration: API-Centric Integration in SAP Integration Suite
  • MCP Gateway: MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of AI

 

Now, let us explore both topics in more detail through a practical modeling perspective.

For the API-centric approach, the following article provides a deeper look at API build and deploy, API governance and lifecycle management:

Part 1: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-on-sap-integration-suite-part-1-build-and-deploy/ba-p/14438357

entry.title: Re: Question regarding account number requirement in SAP...
entry.content:
Hi Soma,

Short answer: A blank bank account only blocks payment when the payment method itself requires bank details; for cash/counter "nofu" payments you should use a payment method (or a manual posting) that does not require bank details.

Is it correct that a blank account number stops payment processing?

Yes, with limits. It depends entirely on how the payment method is classified. In the configuration activity Set Up Payment Methods for Each Country/Region for Payment Transactions (ID 101972), each payment method has a Payment Method Classification and a Required Master Record Specifications section that includes a Bank Details flag (Configuring Payment Methods; KBA 3457437).

I do not have Japanese localization in my starter, so the example is from a different country but still valid:

For bank-transfer methods: if Bank Details is required and the payee business partner has no valid bank account, the automatic payment run (Manage Automatic Payments) cannot select the item, and it is rejected in the proposal. So your understanding is correct for this case only.

For check / cash / counter methods: if Bank Details is not flagged, a blank bank account is not an error and payment proceeds normally.

How should we handle the "nofu" (納付) cash-at-counter case?

Because "nofu" needs no bank account, do not assign a bank-transfer payment method to it. You have two clean Public Cloud options.

Option 1 – Manual posting (recommended for cash slips): Post and clear the open payable directly with the Post Outgoing Payments app (App ID F1612), booking the offset to your cash or a payment/clearing G/L account. Manual clearing performs no bank determination, so a blank bank account is irrelevant (Post Outgoing Payments).

Option 2 – Dedicated non-bank payment method: If you want these items to flow through the payment run for tracking, define a separate payment method for counter/cash payments in activity 101972 with the Bank Details specification left unchecked, then assign it to the relevant open items. The run will process them without requiring an account number.

Verify: Check which method is currently on your "nofu" documents. If it is a bank-transfer type, switching to Option 1 or Option 2 removes the blank-account error.

Summary: The blank-account block is caused by a payment method that requires bank details. For "nofu" cash payments, either clear the open item manually via Post Outgoing Payments (F1612), or set up a dedicated counter/cash payment method without the Bank Details requirement. Do not use a bank-transfer method for these.

Hope it helps.

Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: Streamlining Chile's DTE Document Transmission: A Modular Approach
entry.content:
Hello! 

We're excited to share an important update to how DTE documents are transmitted to Chile's tax authority, SII. This change brings a more efficient, traceable process that better serves organizations operating in Chile.

 

What's Changing?

The previous Chile Send DTE iFlow is being retired. In its place, we're introducing a modular design that distributes responsibilities across multiple flows, each with a clear purpose.

 

Key Improvements

  • Better Traceability: Each flow has a distinct role, making it easier to identify and resolve issues if they arise.
  • Multiple Sender Support: The system now uses the sender identification (RUT Emisor) to select the appropriate certificate for signing documents, enabling support for multiple senders within your organization.
  • Smarter Token Management: The new process calls a Get Token flow, which retrieves and reuses valid tokens. This helps preventing seed exhaustion, a common challenge in high-volume environments.
  • Long Signature Native Support: All signatures are now split automatically to avoid rejection when they exceed the tax authority's limit.
  • Immediate Error Feedback: If a document isn't uploaded with status 0 (the tax authority's OK response), you'll receive an error message immediately. This allows you to identify rejection reasons, make corrections, and resubmit quickly.

 

The New Chile Upload DTE iFlows

  • Chile Upload DTE - 1 Receive (/DTE/Receive): Receives XML from SAP S/4HANA and orchestrates signing and transmission.
  • Chile Upload DTE - 2 Sign (/DTE/Sign): Handles document signing with the appropriate certificates and splits the resulting signatures.
  • Chile Upload DTE - 3 Transmit (/DTE/Transmit): Manages environment routing (test/production) based on the NroResol XML tag, calls Get DTE Token iFlow to retrieve the token, transmits to SII and handles the response.

 

The Enhanced Chile Get DTE Token iFlow

Chile Get DTE Token (/eDoc/Chile/DTE/getToken & Chile/DTE/getToken): Retrieves and manages authentication tokens via Direct Process (/eDoc/Chile/DTE/getToken) or SOAP Request (Chile/DTE/getToken) with improved error handling.

 

Implementation Steps

To activate the new process, consulting teams need to update the SAP Document and Reporting Compliance: Electronic Documents for Chile integration package, deploy the new artifacts, and update the endpoint from /cxf/SendMultipleDTE to /cxf/DTE/Receive.

For more information, see the documents section on SAP Document and Reporting Compliance: Electronic Documents for Chile on SAP Business Accelerator Hub. 

 

This modular approach represents a significant step forward in managing Chilean tax compliance more effectively.

Was this post helpful? Leave a like (kudo) and share the content with your colleagues. Feel free to leave feedback, comments, or questions in the space below.

entry.title: Re: Issue with Multiple Forms Under a Single Output Type
entry.content:
Dear @kkumara,

Yes, the requirement is supported. The screenshot confirms that the system is already generating two separate PICK_LIST output items, differentiated by receiver roles A and B. Therefore, the Receiver determination is already configured correctly.

The issue is in the Form Template determination both output items are currently receiving the same template. SAP does not select multiple templates from rules with identical criteria each output item must determine one template using a distinguishing condition

In Output Parameter Determination -> Manufacturing Order -> Form Template:

  1. Add the Role column using the table settings
  2. Maintain separate rules, for example Role A -> first template, Role B -> second template
  3. Remove or restrict any general rule without a role that currently matches both output items
  4. Activate the rules and test with a newly released production order.

The expected result is two separate PICK_LIST output items and two separate PDFs, each using a different form template. The forms will not be merged into one PDF

More details below:

SAP Help – Output Management for Manufacturing Orders

SAP KBA 3320132 – Different/Multiple Forms for the Same Business Application Document – S/4HANA Cloud

SAP Community – How to Generate Multiple Output Items for Production Orders with Different Form Templates

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: @sap/cds OData V2 Adapter Crashes on Empty Results for Custom-Handled Entities
entry.content:
@SAP/cds OData V2 Adapter Crashes on Empty Results for Custom-Handled Entities

TL;DR

If you have a CAP entity backed by a custom handler (e.g. proxying a remote OData service like SuccessFactors, rather than a local DB), returning an empty array [] from that handler on a /$count request crashes the whole server process with an uncaught TypeError. The fix is a one-line guard in @SAP/cds's internal _getCountFromResult function.

Environment

Package Version

@SAP/cds9.9.1
Node.js(as used in cov2ap / @SAP/cds-odata-v2-adapter-proxy runtime)

The Crash

[cds] [ERROR] ❗️Uncaught TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading '$count')
    at _getCountFromResult (.../@sap/cds/libx/odata/middleware/read.js:129:75)
    at .../@sap/cds/libx/odata/middleware/read.js:291:61
    at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:105:5)
[cds] [ERROR] ❗️server shutdown ...❗️

Note the last line: this isn't just a failed request — it's an uncaught exception that shuts down the entire server process. A single malformed $count sub-request from a client can take down the whole service.

Root Cause

Trace the failure to read.js line 129:

const _getCountFromResult = result =>
  (Array.isArray(result)
    ? (result[0].$count ?? 0)
    : (result.$count ?? 0))

The logic assumes that if a handler's result is an array, the count metadata will live on the array's first element (result[0].$count) — this mirrors how CAP's built-in DB layer attaches paging metadata internally.

That assumption breaks the moment the array is empty:

  • Array.isArray(result) → true
  • result[0] → undefined (no first element exists)
  • undefined.$count → TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading '$count')

Why this matters for custom/remote-backed entities

This code path assumes every array-returning handler behaves like the generic DB read path, which always guarantees at least metadata-bearing structure even for zero rows. But for entities backed by custom handlers calling external systems (SuccessFactors, other remote OData services, etc.), a legitimate "no records found" result is just a plain [] — there is no framework-provided mechanism to safely attach $count to an empty array, because there's no index 0 to attach it to.

In practice, this means: any custom-handled entity that can legitimately return zero rows will crash the server on a $count request, with no clean workaround available from the handler side.

Reproduction

  1. Register a custom READ handler for an entity that proxies a remote system:
    srv.on('READ', 'cust_MyEntity', async (req) => {  const result = await remoteApi.fetch(req.query);  return result; // could legitimately be []});
  2. Trigger a $count sub-request against that entity (UI5 list bindings do this automatically, e.g. for growing/paged tables):
    GET /cust_MyEntity/$count
  3. When the remote system returns zero matching records, the handler returns [].
  4. CAP's OData V2 adapter crashes trying to compute the count → server shutdown.

Suggested Fix

The fix is a minimal, backward-compatible guard — only attempt to read result[0] when the array actually has an element:

const _getCountFromResult = result =>
  ((Array.isArray(result) && result.length > 0)
    ? (result[0].$count ?? 0)
    : (result.$count ?? 0));

Why this works

  • Non-empty array → unchanged behavior, still reads result[0].$count.
  • Empty array ([]) → falls through to the else branch, result.$count is evaluated on the array itself. Since a plain empty array has no .$count property, ?? 0 safely resolves to 0 instead of throwing.
  • Non-array result (e.g. a plain number for count-only requests) → unchanged, still reads result.$count ?? 0.

This preserves all existing framework behavior for DB-backed reads while fixing the crash for any handler that can return [].

Current Workarounds (Application-Level)

Since @SAP/cds is a closed-source package published via SAP's official channels (bugs are reported via SAP Support Portal, not a public GitHub PR), teams hitting this today need mitigations on the application side while waiting for an official fix:

1. Handle count-only requests explicitly, returning a plain number instead of an array:

srv.on('READ', 'cust_MyEntity', async (req) => {
  if (req.query.SELECT?.count) {
    const count = await getCountFromRemote(req.query);
    return count ?? 0; // plain number — never hits the array branch
  }
  const result = await remoteApi.fetch(req.query);
  return result;
});

2. Add a safety net so a single bad request can't take down the whole server:

cds.on('bootstrap', app => {
  app.use((err, req, res, next) => {
    console.error('CDS request error (prevented crash):', err);
    if (!res.headersSent) {
      res.status(500).json({ error: { message: 'Internal Server Error' } });
    }
  });
});

Neither of these is a substitute for the actual framework fix — they reduce blast radius while the root cause remains in @SAP/cds itself.

Reporting

Since @SAP/cds is closed-source, this can't be raised as a community GitHub PR. If you're hitting this, raise an incident via the SAP Support Portal against the CAP Node.js runtime component, referencing:

  • Package/version: @SAP/cds@9.9.1
  • File/line: libx/odata/middleware/read.js:129, function _getCountFromResult
  • The suggested one-line fix above
  • A minimal repro (custom handler + entity returning [] on a $count request)

If you've hit this same crash with a custom or remote-backed CAP entity, it's worth checking whether your handlers guard against empty-array $count requests — it's an easy one to miss until it takes production down.

entry.title: Re: Lock PO net price in PO document but cannot find PO t...
entry.content:
Hi @KUO_WANRU 

Short answer: In Public Cloud, the Manage Purchase Orders / Create Purchase Order Fiori apps only fully support document types that are equivalent to standard NB. Because your Z‑type carries a non‑standard field selection key ($DE1), its POs are treated as "Advanced" and are excluded from the standard Fiori app. Align the field selection key with NB in Define Document Types to make the type appear.

Why the type is missing in Manage Purchase Orders

Cause: A custom PO type must be copied from NB with all dependent entries, including the field selection key. If a PO has features that the Fiori app does not support (a non‑standard field selection key being one of them), it is flagged as an "Advanced" Purchase Order and can only be created, edited, or displayed in the classic apps Create/Change Purchase Order – Advanced (your ME21N route) — not in the standard Manage Purchase Orders app. Reference: Manage Purchase Orders – SAP Help (Public Edition)

The setting that fixes it

In Manage Your Solution → Configure Your Solution → Sourcing and Procurement → Operational Procurement, open the activity Define Document Types for Purchase Orders (SSCUI 102909). For your Z‑type, set the FieldSelKey column to the same value used by standard NB, and confirm the type was created as a full copy of NB. After that, the type is offered in the Purchase Order Type value help of the standard apps. References: Manage Purchase Orders App FAQ – Document Types and KBA 2685355 – How to create a custom Purchasing Document Type in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

Keeping your net price / amount lock

If you locked net price and amount only by assigning the non‑standard field selection key $DE1, switching the key back to NB will remove that lock. In Public Cloud, the supported way to lock those fields while staying compatible with the Fiori app is field control via BAdI, not a custom field selection key: use Change of Field Control for Purchase Order MM_PUR_S4_PO_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE (item level) — or the header variant MM_PUR_S4_PO_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE_H — in Custom Fields and Logic, and set Net Price / Net Amount to read‑only under your own conditions. Reference: KBA 3268822 – Field Control in Purchase Orders and Purchase Requisitions

Summary

Set your custom type's field selection key equal to standard NB in Define Document Types for Purchase Orders (SSCUI 102909) so the Manage / Create Purchase Order apps recognize it, then re‑implement the net price/amount lock through BAdI MM_PUR_S4_PO_FLDCNTRL_SIMPLE (item) / _SIMPLE_H (header) instead of the $DE1 key. Test with a newly created PO of that type.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: Adobe LiveCycle Designer - "usehref" fails to resolve master (SOMU_FORM_MASTER_A4_SG) for SIPT_CI_S
entry.content:
Environment:
- Adobe LiveCycle Designer 11.0.9 (SAP version)
- Form: SIPT_CI_SDBIL_E (Billing Document output form, PT-PT localization)
- Data connection: SIPT_CI_SDBIL_E.xsd
- Master referenced via usehref: SOMU_FORM_MASTER_A4_SG.xdp,
target som($template.Form.Landscape_ItemList)

 

I downloaded the layout (.xdp) and the corresponding data schema (.xsd)
for the standard Billing Document form SIPT_CI_SDBIL_E, along with the
referenced master form SOMU_FORM_MASTER_A4_SG.xdp, all placed in the
same local folder. When opening SIPT_CI_SDBIL_E.xdp in LiveCycle
Designer, the Design View / Project Preview renders completely blank,
and the Log shows the following messages:

1) "Incorrect value: '.\SOMU_FORM_MASTER_A4_SG.xdp#som($template.Form.
Landscape_ItemList)' for attribute 'usehref' of element 'subform' ''.
Default will be used."

2) "PageSet elements with infinite occurrence must contain at least
one contentArea"

3) "Could not select new pageArea: no matching occurrence found or
occurrence error. Cancelling layout."

What I already checked/tried:
- Confirmed all three files (SIPT_CI_SDBIL_E.xdp, .xsd, and
SOMU_FORM_MASTER_A4_SG.xdp) are in the same folder.

 


- Opened SOMU_FORM_MASTER_A4_SG.xdp directly (standalone) and confirmed
the Hierarchy DOES contain the "Landscape_ItemList" fragment, with
mst1 (FirstPage, cntMain1) and mst2 (SubsequentPage, cntMain2) master
pages defined, each with a content area — see attached screenshot.

Despite the referenced fragment structurally existing in the master
file (as shown in the screenshot), the usehref resolution still fails
and falls back to default, leaving the pageSet without any resolved
contentArea, which then causes the layout engine to cancel rendering
entirely.

Question:
What else could cause usehref to fail resolving a fragment that is
visibly present in the target master file (same folder, matching
filename)? Could this be caused by:
- A version/build mismatch between the form and the master (even
though both were exported around the same time)?
- Some internal ID/checksum stored in the .xdp that must match between
form and master beyond just the filename and SOM path?
- A missing intermediate fragment or dependency not included in my
download?

Any guidance on how to correctly export a form + its dependent master
fragment(s) as a consistent, self-contained package from SFP (or the
equivalent app in S/4HANA Cloud) so that local preview in Designer
works reliably would also be very welcome.

entry.title: Table MDMA issue while conversion from ECC to S/4 HANA.
entry.content:
Hello Team,

Please help us understand the possible cause of this issue.

At the start of the SUM Extraction Phase, the MDMA table contained no records. Later, during the Final SI Check Resolution phase, the program MRP_AREA_STORAGE_LOC_MIGRATION was executed for MRP Area Storage Location Assignment, resulting in approximately 2 million records being created in MDMA.

Subsequently, during the DOWNTIME_DMO_REPLAY phase, MDMA table extraction failed, but no detailed error information is available.

Could this failure be related to the fact that MDMA was empty during the initial Extraction Phase and was populated only afterward? Has anyone encountered a similar issue or can advise on the likely root cause?

Any guidance would be appreciated.

Thanks

entry.title: The AI Skills Library Is Live: Discover, Share, and Build AI Skills Together
entry.content:
The AI Skills Library is SAP's public hub for reusable AI skills, plugins, MCP servers, and marketplaces. Explore existing solutions, share your own innovations, and collaborate with developers and business users to shape the future of enterprise AI.

The AI Skills Library Is Live - Help Shape What's Next

Whether you're a developer automating technical workflows or a business user streamlining everyday tasks, you can learn from the work of others and share your own expertise with the community.

That's the idea behind SAP's AI Skills Library: an open and growing collection of reusable AI skills, plugins, MCP servers, and marketplaces. Explore what's already available, contribute your own work, and help shape the ecosystem as it evolves.

What Is a Skill?

A skill captures a workflow once, so AI assistants such as Joule or Claude can execute it consistently. Instead of repeatedly explaining how a task should be done, you define the process once and reuse it whenever needed. Skills make recurring workflows reusable, helping AI assistants perform common tasks more consistently and efficiently.

What Is the AI Skills Library?

The AI Skills Library (skills.cloud.sap) is a public directory where you can:

  • Discover reusable AI skills, MCP servers, plugins, or marketplaces
  • Install skills into supported AI environments
  • Share your own skills, plugins, or marketplaces with others
  • Learn from how other developers and business users solve recurring problems

Contributions are managed through a public GitHub repository, making openness and transparency a core part of the platform.

All published skills are clearly labeled:

  • SAP-Certified Skills – reviewed against defined quality standards
  • Community Skills – created and shared by developers and business users
  • Both contribute to building a diverse and growing ecosystem.

Getting Started

The AI Skills Library first launched at SAP Sapphire and continues to evolve with every new contribution.

Depending on your environment, skills can be installed in different ways. Installation instructions and technical documentation are available in the documentation. Alternatively, check out the AI Skills Library Tutorial.

What Can You Use Skills For?

Skills and plugins can support a wide range of repeatable tasks, from coding and content creation to meeting preparation and reporting.

Whether you're reviewing code, summarizing meetings, preparing customer conversations, or automating routine business processes, skills help AI assistants reuse workflows instead of recreating them every time.

If you find yourself repeating the same process regularly, it may be a good candidate for a skill. And if you've built something useful for yourself, there's a good chance it will help someone else too.

Help Grow the Community

The AI Skills Library is still growing, and that's intentional.

Rather than waiting until every possible use case exists, we're opening the platform early so developers, partners, customers, and business users can help shape it together.

You can already:

  • Explore existing skills
  • Publish your own skills or plugins
  • Contribute through GitHub
  • Exchange ideas and ask questions on SAP Community

Every contribution helps expand the ecosystem and gives others a starting point for their own AI workflows.

The platform is open. The repository is public. Now it's your turn - what skill will you create first?

entry.title: From Resolution to Intelligence: Reimagining Case Closure with Automation
entry.content:
Transforming Global Customer Support, One Resolved Case at a Time

Every day, Support Engineers resolve customer issues through chat. But even after the customer has their answer, the work isn't over.

Today, engineers still spend valuable time performing post-resolution activities updating PULSE, selecting the appropriate KBA, assigning error categories, adding external information, and finally closing the case. While these tasks are essential, they are repetitive, time-consuming, and reduce the time available to focus on what matters most: helping customers.

The Opportunity

What if the case could complete its journey automatically once the customer issue is resolved?

Our idea introduces Automatic Case Closure, an intelligent post-resolution process that streamlines the end of the support lifecycle.

When a customer accepts a solution or when there is no further engagement after a configurable period the system automatically:

  • Closes the case.

  • Captures the relevant resolution details in PULSE.

  • Applies predefined KBAs, error categories, and external information using configurable templates.

  • Creates a consistent and structured knowledge record with minimal manual effort.

The result is a faster, smarter, and more standardized support process.

Why This Matters

The value extends far beyond simply closing cases.

Every resolved interaction contains valuable knowledge. By automatically capturing and structuring this information, we create a growing repository of reusable intelligence that benefits the entire support organization.

This enables:

  • Reduced administrative effort for Support Engineers.

  • Faster case lifecycle completion.

  • Improved reporting accuracy and governance.

  • Consistent knowledge capture across teams and regions.

  • More time for engineers to focus on solving customer problems.

Building the Foundation for AI

One of the most exciting aspects of this idea is its long-term potential.

Structured resolution data captured in PULSE can become the foundation for Autonomous Agents and future AI-driven capabilities. Instead of repeatedly solving similar issues from scratch, AI can learn from historical resolutions, recognize recurring patterns, and recommend proven solutions.

In other words, every resolved case becomes an opportunity to improve the next customer interaction.

A Scalable Vision

This solution is designed with Global Customer Support in mind.

By using configurable templates and standardized workflows, the automation can be adapted to different regional or business requirements while maintaining consistency across the organization.

It is:

  • Desirable – reducing repetitive work and improving the experience for both engineers and customers.

  • Feasible – leveraging existing capabilities such as PULSE and current support workflows.

  • Viable – improving operational efficiency and data quality.

  • Scalable – applicable across teams, regions, and products.

  • Strategically Aligned – supporting our vision of intelligent automation and AI-enabled customer support.

Looking Ahead

Automation isn't just about reducing clicks.

It's about enabling our people to spend more time solving complex customer challenges while allowing technology to handle repetitive administrative tasks.

By transforming post-resolution activities into an intelligent, automated workflow, we're not only improving efficiency today we're creating a smarter, knowledge-driven support ecosystem for tomorrow.

Because every resolved case shouldn't just end a conversation it should help solve the next one.

SAP CRM Sales integration with SAP CRM

entry.title: SAP Service Cloud Q2 '26 Release Highlights
entry.content:
SAP Service Cloud Q2 2026 Release Highlights

Explore a selection of our new features in this blog post and join our webcasts for live demos and deeper insights SAP Service Cloud Version 2 updates. Register for the Q2 release webcast here.

The momentum from SAP Sapphire 2026 continues to energize the service community. We are looking forward to bringing autonomous service to life with autonomous AI agents and assistants.  While we look ahead to these innovations, we're also continuing to deliver enhancements to your day-to-day work. This quarter brings a range of updates across SAP Service Cloud designed to simplify everyday service tasks and help service teams spend less time on process and more time on customers.

Email Editor Built For the AI-first World


Email is one of the biggest opportunities for AI to improve productivity.  A new, native email editor is now available in SAP Service Cloud—built from the ground up for an AI-first world.  Today, it gives service reps a modern, streamlined writing experience with the formatting and productivity features they expect. AI capabilities are coming next, built directly into the email experience — helping teams respond faster while keeping every response personal and on point.

Better Customer Service with Voice AI Through the Parloa Partnership

 

At Sapphire, we announced an expanded partnership with Parloa, bringing together conversational AI and SAP Service Cloud to help organizations deliver more seamless customer service experiences. By connecting AI-powered customer interactions with SAP business processes and data, companies can provide faster, more contextual support across voice and digital channels. Available today, this partnership represents an important step toward more intelligent and increasingly autonomous service operations.


 Sales Objects: Broader Support in the Service Agent Workspace

 

Service interactions often reveal new customer needs and valuable buying signals. Based on strong customer demand, your service reps can now create leads, opportunities, appointments, and sales orders directly from Customer Hub—without leaving their workspace. This helps organizations bridge the gap between service and sales, deliver a more seamless customer experience and capture cross-sell and upsell opportunities.

 

 

  

 Case Management: Enhanced Compliance, Usability and Quality


Case management now includes new capabilities that improve compliance, usability, and data quality.
- Organizations can now define data retention rules by case type, enabling more granular governance and compliance with business and regulatory requirements.

 

 

- Agents can copy sub-cases while preserving their relationship to the parent case, making it easier to manage complex service scenarios.

 


- New capabilities such as Consistency Check for Cases help service reps identify and resolve data inconsistencies, ensuring cases are complete, accurate, and ready to progress through the service process.

 

 


Integrations: Service Contracts and Service Orders


Show Service Contract and Item Information in Service Orders
Service reps can now view associated service contract and contract item information directly within service orders. This enhancement provides service teams with easier access to customer agreement details while processing service orders.

Service Contract determination
Service Cloud can now automatically determine the relevant service contract when a service order is created. This helps service teams quickly identify applicable customer agreements, entitlements, and contract coverage without manual searches, ensuring that service activities are aligned with the customer's contractual commitments.

Embedded AI: Simpler Administration And Richer Insights for Similar Cases

You can now discover and manage AI capabilities more easily with a redesigned, tile-based configuration interface. Admins can quickly access feature settings and gain a clearer overview of available AI capabilities. For service agents, Similar Case Recommendations now provide additional case details directly in the recommendation view, enabling a better understanding of recommended cases and helping agents quickly identify the most useful information.

 

 

Service order: improved usability and extensions

Working with service orders is now easier with enhancements that improve visibility and navigation.
Visual filters help service reps quickly focus on relevant service orders and detailed item views provide greater transparency into service order content. You can also create home page KPI cards for service orders and service quotes.

 


Enhanced integration capabilities help connect service orders across business applications. Administrators can create external links to related records in external systems using external ID's. We have also added support for multiple external SAP systems providing greater flexibility for organizations operating across connected SAP landscapes.


 

Utilities: SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud utilities add-on


As announced at Sapphire, a new add-on for utilities is now available, covering sales processes in addition to the service processes already supported. This new add-on helps utility companies reduce friction between sales and service, increase efficiency and elevate customer experience. If you are interested in this topic, watch this webinar: Utilities CX Reloaded: The New Sales Process Explained

 

 

More capabilities to explore

This release also brings a range of additional improvements worth exploring. 
Administrators can now sign in as another user to troubleshoot issues faster and without friction.
Analytics capabilities have been extended, with support for embedding SAP Analytics Cloud widgets directly into daily workflows and the ability to use custom service entities as data sources.
Approval processes are now more flexible and reliable, with configurable approval and rejection reasons and automatic approval settings to keep workflows moving.

In our upcoming Q2 release webcast we will take a closer look at these and many other capabilities. Looking forward to seeing you there!

Additional resources: 
Sign up for our Sales and Service Newsletter to stay up to date with the latest information. 
Read the CX Q2 release blog
