entry.title: New POD 2.0 Framework: Review by Exception
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series on SAP Digital Manufacturing new POD 2.0 framework, the new generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing worker UIs.

New POD 2.0 Framework Blogs

  1. The New Generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing Worker UIs
  2. Template-Based Design Instead of POD Types
  3. Visual WYSIWYG Designer
  4. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 1
  5. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 2
  6. Custom UI Dialogs and Calling Production Processes
  7. Dynamic PODs, How to Dynamically Show, Hide, Enable, and Disable UI Elements
  8. Other Business Capabilities Enabled by POD 2.0
  9. Interactive Value Journey (IVJ)
  10. POD Notifications
  11. Tips and Tricks
  12. Accessing PODs 2.0
  13. Implementing Data Scan for Barcode-enabled Component Consumption
  14. Electronic Batch Records (EBR)
  15. Certificate Check in POD 2.0
  16. Review by Exception

Introduction

Review by Exception process is used to review, approve, and reject deviations in the manufacturing process.

In the context of Electronic Batch Record (EBR), deviation is an instance where the process does not conform to the specified requirements, and it is handled as a nonconformance.

By the end of this guide, you will have a fully configured POD 2.0 setup that supports nonconformance review, electronic signature collection, approval and rejection workflows, and detailed activity logging for compliance and traceability purposes.

Business Value and Outcomes

  • Reduce batch review effort and cycle time
    • Exception-based review removes “noise,” focusing QA and production teams on only the records that need attention, shortening batch disposition timelines.
  • Strengthen compliance and reduce risk
    • Electronic signatures and controlled, exception-driven workflows support regulatory requirements (for example, cGMP, FDA 21 CFR Part 11), reducing findings during audits.
  • Improve right‑first‑time and product quality
    • Tight integration with non‑conformance handling improves detection, escalation, and closure of deviations, reducing rework and scrap.
  • Increase throughput and lower cost to produce
    • By simplifying the review process and accelerating exception resolution, plants can release batches faster and reduce labor effort.
  • Enhance traceability and accountability
    • A complete, electronic record of exceptions, decisions, and approvals supports internal governance and partner/customer transparency.

Prerequisites

  • You're assigned the NC_Review role to be able to review nonconformances.
  • You're assigned the NC_Approve role to be able to approve or reject nonconformances.
  • You're assigned the Electronic_Signature_Sign role.

  • You have set Relevant for Electronic Batch Record to Yes for the corresponding nonconformance groups in the Manage Nonconformance Groups app.

 
POD Setup
 
Add a new Icon Tab Filter to your POD Icon Tab Bar:

 

Adjust the look and feel accordingly:

From Plugins tab, as a Horizontal Box with 100% Height and two Vertical Boxes with 70% and 30% Width respectively:

On each Vertical Box, add Nonconformance Data Tree and Nonconformance Activity Log plugins, so they show side-by-side:

In Pages tab, click + and Add Dialog:

Name it whatever you like, add Signature plugin and copy the plugin ID on the right-hand side:

Select Sign button, and then click on Events > Press > Edit Actions on the right-hand side panel, then click + and add CloseDialog action, so this dialog is automatically closed right after the electronic signature is captured.

Back to OrderDetails page, select the Nonconformance Data Tree plugin and enable:

  • Display Close Nonconformance Button
  • Display Open Nonconformance Button
  • Review by Exeption Mode

Paste the Signature plugin ID copied on the step before to Electronic Signature Plugin ID field.

On the right-hand side, under Events > Electronic Signature Dialog, click Edit Actions, click +, add a ShowDialog action and configure it to load the newly created dialog with the Signature plugin:

Save and run it!

Learn more at: Review by Exception Mode.

Run 

Configure and use Nonconformance Data Entry plugin to create a Nonconformance from a Nonconformance Group where Relevant for Electronic Batch Record is set to Yes:

Now, open your newly created POD and the Review by Exception tab, to see all the logged Nonconformances against that SFC/Charge. On the right-hand side, at Nonconformance Activity Log, you will see step-by-step details of all the activities this nonconformance has been through.

The Nonconformances created with Codes from Groups with Relevant for Electronic Batch Record set to Yes will have a green mark at the beginning of the row, as they are relevant for the Review by Exception process.

Select one of them, and click Close Nonconformance:

With the nonconformance closed, click Review Nonconformance to review it:

Which will open the Electronic Signature popup for signing:

Once the electronic signature is collected, the dialog will auto close, and the plugins will show latest Reviewed status.

Now, click either Approve Nonconformance or Reject Nonconformance:

And collect the electronic signature once again:

Once the electronic signature is collected, the dialog will auto close, and the plugins will show latest Approved status.

At this point, you can still Reject Nonconformance back, and restart the cycle.

The full Nonconformance Activity Log is available on the right-hand side, and can be used for audit trail purposes.

Conclusion

With the new POD 2.0 framework, implementing a complete Review by Exception workflow becomes a highly configurable, low-code exercise. By combining the Nonconformance Data Tree, Nonconformance Activity Log, custom dialogs, and the Signature plugin, you can create a compliant review and approval process directly within the operator interface.

The solution demonstrated in this blog enables manufacturing and quality teams to:

  • Review nonconformances associated with an SFC or batch/charge.

  • Capture electronic signatures during review and approval steps.

  • Approve or reject deviations while maintaining regulatory compliance.

  • Maintain a complete audit trail through the Nonconformance Activity Log.

  • Support Electronic Batch Record processes with traceable review actions.

This scenario also highlights one of the key strengths of POD 2.0: the ability to orchestrate complex business workflows through configuration rather than custom development. The same approach can be extended to additional quality and compliance processes, making POD 2.0 a powerful foundation for modern, paperless manufacturing operations.

 

Stay tuned for more! Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: SAP TM - How to configure the system to propagate Commodity Codes from Material Master to TM Freight
entry.content:
Overview

Commodity codes are classification codes used globally to identify goods and services for customs, trade compliance, and transportation purposes. In SAP, the commodity code is stored in the plant segment of the material master (field MARC-STAWN) and plays a critical role in foreign trade, preference processing, and logistics execution. 

A common business requirement is to ensure that commodity codes maintained in the material master are correctly propagated to SAP Transportation Management (TM) freight documents — including Freight Orders, Freight Bookings, and Freight Units — so that cross-border shipments carry accurate classification data for customs and carrier compliance.

This blog walks through the end-to-end configuration required to achieve this propagation, from material master setup through TM classification and freight document verification.

Prerequisites

Before beginning configuration, ensure the following are in place:

  • Commodity codes are defined in the system. In SAP S/4HANA, commodity codes are stored in tables /SAPSLL/CLSNR (Trade Classification Number) and /SAPSLL/CLSNRT (descriptions). In classic ERP, they are maintained in table T604. 
  • Numbering schemes are configured in TM Customizing under Master Data → Classification of Goods → Define Commodity Codes. SAP delivers the Standard Transportation Commodity Code (STCC) as default numbering scheme content, but custom schemes can be defined.  

  • Material master records exist with the commodity code field (MARC-STAWN) populated at the plant segment level. Note that it is possible — though generally undesirable — to store different commodity codes in different plant segments for the same material.  
  • Authorization object YYPR_STAWN is assigned to users responsible for maintaining commodity codes, if the control table YTY01 switch (ID = 0017, Par1 = 0045) is activated. Only users with this authorization will be able to change the commodity code field.  
  • TM integration is active between SAP S/4HANA logistics documents (SD/LE/MM) and SAP TM, with TM Control Keys and Logistics Integration Profiles configured.  

 

Configuration Steps

Step 1: Ensure Commodity Codes Are Correctly Maintained in the Material Master

The commodity code field is stored in the plant segment of the material master (MARC-STAWN). Maintenance of this field is resource-intensive given the volume of materials and codes. SAP provides the report YYSTAWN (called via transaction YYSTAWN) to manage this efficiently. 

  • For initial assignments, a manual assignment is unavoidable for the first time. Subsequent changes resulting from data updates can be handled more efficiently using the YYSTAWN program. 
  • To change individual commodity codes in the material master, use the Convert commodity codes radio button in the YYSTAWN report. Enter the expiring or invalid codes in the Commodity code old fields and the newly assigned codes (validated against table T604) in the Commodity code new fields. New codes are not written back until you click Save; change documents are written.  
  • If more than 9 conversion rules are needed, upload a conversion file into table YYCONVERT_STAWN using YYSTAWN_UPLOAD (accessible via transaction SA38 or transaction YYSTAWN_UPLOAD). 
  • To detect inconsistencies, the YYSTAWN report also provides analyses for: materials with no commodity code, materials with invalid codes, materials with different codes across plants, recently changed materials, and materials with 6-digit codes. 

Important: A change of the commodity code in the material master can have extensive consequences for preference determination. All preference calculations containing the affected material must be recalculated before the next shipment. 

Step 2: Configure Commodity Code Numbering Schemes and Product Classification in TM

Once commodity codes are correctly maintained in the material master, configure TM to recognize and use them:

  1. Define Numbering Schemes for Commodity Codes Navigate to TM Customizing and define the numbering scheme relevant to your transportation mode (e.g., STCC for North American rail, or a custom scheme for EU trade). You can assign a validity period to the numbering scheme content to accommodate changes in code length or structure over time.  

  1. Assign Numbering Scheme to Country/Region (if applicable) In TM configuration, assign the numbering scheme for commodity codes to the relevant country or region. 

  1. Classify Products Using the TM App Use the Manage Commodity Codes for Transportation app (App ID: F7150) to create entries for commodity codes under the relevant numbering scheme content. You can add descriptions, define validity periods, and add descriptions in multiple languages. 

Then use the Classify Products – Commodity Codes for Transportation app to assign commodity codes to products (materials). Once assigned, the commodity codes will appear in Freight Units, Freight Orders, and Freight Bookings whenever a classified product is transported. 

 

Step 3: Configure Propagation from FU to Freight Documents

In SAP TM, the Freight Unit acts as the primary source of truth for commodity code classification at the cargo item level. For that need to customize the FU to be relevant for take the Commodity from customizing;

Also check the Freight Order type:

The system automatically copies commodity codes from the Freight Unit to the corresponding Freight Order or Freight Booking.  

Verification

To confirm that commodity codes are correctly propagating to TM freight documents, perform the following checks:

Check the Freight Order / Freight Booking

    • In   SAP Fiori, navigate to Freight Order Management → Change Freight Order (or Change Air/Ocean Freight Booking).
    • Open the detail data for a main cargo item and navigate to the Commodity Codes tab page.

Check Freight Unit Items

    • Navigate to the detailed view of a Freight Unit.
    • In the Items tab, select a product and then choose Commodity Codes to display the commodity codes assigned to that product. 

Validate Material Master Consistency

    • Run the YYSTAWN report analyses to confirm there are no materials with missing, invalid, or inconsistent commodity codes across plants before processing shipments. 

Summary

Propagating commodity codes from the material master to TM freight documents requires a coordinated setup across material master maintenance, TM numbering scheme configuration, product classification, and forwarding order management. Key points to remember:

  • Commodity codes are stored at the plant segment level in the material master (MARC-STAWN) and must be consistently maintained. 
  • TM uses its own numbering scheme framework; products must be classified in TM using the Classify Products app for codes to appear on freight documents. 
  • Changes to commodity codes in the material master may require recalculation of preference determinations for affected materials. 

Important Notes

3778673 - How to configure the system to propagate Commodity Codes from Material Master to TM Freight Documents - SAP for Me

2681467 - Commodity Code propagation to Freight Documents in SAP Transportation Management - SAP for Me

Hope that can help you.

Thanks, and regards

Rogerio Lima.

entry.title: 2026 SAP Quality Awards for Customer Success CEE | Номінуйте ваш SAP-проєкт
entry.content:
Номінаційний період 2026 SAP Quality Awards for Customer Success CEE відкрито — дедлайн 31 серпня 2026 року. Якщо ваша компанія успішно реалізувала SAP-проєкт — номінуйтесь вже сьогодні!

Україна вже на карті переможців
За 2024–2025 роки українські компанії здобули 7 нагород SAP Quality Awards for Customer Success CEE (Central & Eastern Europe).🏆✨

2025 — SAP Quality Awards for Customer Success CEE

  • Оператор ГТС України — Business Transformation – Enterprise Solutions (також отримав спеціальну відзнаку SAP Quality Director Award)
  • Novus Ukraine — Business Transformation – Enterprise Solutions
  • Metinvest — Business Transformation – Functional Transformation
  • Business Evolution — Rapid Time to Value

2024 — SAP Quality Awards for Customer Success CEE

  • Дарниця — Business Transformation – Corporate Solutions
  • Національна служба здоров’я України (НСЗУ) — Business Transformation – Corporate Solutions
  • Metinvest — Business Transformation – Functional Transformation

Це не випадковість — це підтвердження того, що українські підприємства реалізують проєкти світового рівня навіть в умовах воєнного часу. Хто буде наступним у 2026? 

Що таке SAP Quality Awards for Customer Success?
Престижна нагорода SAP, яка відзначає клієнтів за видатні результати впровадження SAP-рішень. Переможці отримують:

  • Визнання в SAP-спільноті та публічне висвітлення в медіа і пресі
  • Участь у церемонії нагородження
  • Офіційний логотип переможця для використання в комунікаціях компанії

Три категорії для номінації
1) Business Transformation — масштабні трансформаційні проєкти
Два з трьох критеріїв: понад 500 користувачів | понад 6 місяців | понад 900 людино-днів

2) Rapid Time to Value — швидкі та ефективні впровадження
Два з трьох критеріїв: менше 600 користувачів | менше 7 місяців | менше 1 000 людино-днів

3) Sustainability — проєкти із вимірюваним впливом на сталий розвиток
Використання SAP Sustainability Portfolio + вимірюваний вплив на Цілі сталого розвитку ООН

Хто може подати заявку?

  • Будь-яка організація з успішним впровадженням будь-якого SAP-продукту
  • Проєкт запущений у продуктивну експлуатацію від 2 до 18 місяців до дати подачі заявки
  • Одна компанія може подати заявки в кількох категоріях — за умови, що кожна заявка стосується окремого SAP-проєкту

Як подати заявку?

  1. Відкрийте анкету з номінаційного порталу
  2. Заповніть усі питання англійською або німецькою мовою
  3. Подайте номінацію онлайн до 31 серпня 2026

Детальна інформація та посилання на номінаційний портал: SAP Quality Awards for Customer Success

Якщо ваш проєкт відповідає критеріям — не зволікайте.🏆
Нехай 2026 додасть ще більше українських імен до списку переможців. 🇺🇦

Update your fragment to use macros:Table instead of macrosTable:Table

<core:FragmentDefinition
    xmlns="sap.m"
    xmlns:core="sap.ui.core"
    xmlns:macros="sap.fe.macros"
... >
<macros:Table
...>
</macros:Table>

entry.title: Re: SAP GUI for Windows 8.10 Beta Program – Program finished (update from 28th of November 2025)
entry.content:
Installer

@matthias_blessinger1: Yes, the installer contains GuiXT 2026 Q2 2.

@All: The initial installer (5.06 GB unpacked) also contains:
- Adobe LiveCycle Designer 11.0 Patch 32 (1.07 GB unpacked)
- SAP GUI for Java 8.10 Revision 12 (2.52 GB unpacked)
IMHO, including these components in the main Windows installer is a waste of download bandwidth and storage space for a typical user.
They are available as separate downloads. No need to include them here.

Themes

@kristenbarge: The following themes are available:
Belize (Belize High Contrast Black, Belize High Contrast White, Belize)
Blue Crystal
Classic
Corbu
Horizon (Horizon Dark, Horizon High Contrast Black, Horizon High Contrast White, Horizon)
Match Microsoft Windows theme
Quartz (Quartz Dark, Quartz High Contrast Black, Quartz High Contrast White, Quartz)
SAP Signature (SAP Signature High Contrast Black, SAP Signature)

@All: The following is, of course, somewhat subjective.

The Horizon theme is not a major game changer. It still follows the same approach as the other new, Fiori-style themes.
The icons in particular are still really impractical. They are difficult to scan and use in daily work, especially for technical and power users.
IMHO, the last truly usable icons were those used in the Corbu theme. The newer themes have very little color or visual distinction.
In practice, not many of the people I work with use them.

The new themes may be suitable for someone who opens SAP, approves one request, and logs out after five minutes.
However, for users who use SAP as a main part of their daily work, these themes are visually unclear and difficult to use.
Too little information is visually distinguishable at a glance, making transactions more difficult to scan and navigate.
Based on my experience, it feels as though the new themes have been designed primarily with occasional users in mind, at the expense of usability for those who spend a significant part of their working day in SAP.

The sad thing is that when I checked SAP GUI for Java 8.10, its theme looked significantly more useful than the corresponding Windows version:

entry.title: Re: POD 2.0: Data Scan for Barcode-enabled Process Order...
entry.content:
Hi @ShK,

Indeed, for Order POD execution and Process Orders, you need to use a different service than the ones for Work Center POD and Production Orders.

Regarding BOM, you can call Retrieve Order Detail service to retrieve it, and use it as input for Goods Issue service, as shown in the example below.

Regarding Operation Activity being empty for Order POD, I recently heard about this issue, and understood it should be fixed during 2608 release cycle. In any case, I suggest you raise a ticket to get proper support.

Meanwhile and alternatively, you can evaluate calling one of the existing services to read the SFC/Charge details, including the current step and get the current Operation Activity / Phase, same approach as reading the BOM above.

Or, you can add Operation Activity / Phase to the barcode and map it as a Data Qualifier for now.

Hope it helps! Br, Manoel

entry.title: SAP Sales and Service Cloud V2: How SAP Is Rebuilding the Future of Customer Experience
entry.content:
SAP has quietly reset the clock on its CRM platform. SAP Sales Cloud Version 2 (V2) isn't a facelift or a routine upgrade — it's a ground-up rebuild, engineered natively on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). With Joule AI agents now generally available, deal intelligence based on real activity data, and a completely reworked forecasting experience, V2 represents where SAP believes customer experience is headed. If you're kicking off a new SAP CX project today, this is where it should start.

Here's a snapshot of what that rebuild looks like in numbers:

Note: OData V4 currently has restricted availability. Getting access requires opening a case against the component CEC-CRM-CZM-EXT.

Why a Rebuild, Not an Upgrade

SAP's CRM story stretches back decades — from the original WebUI, through ABAP-based CRM On-Premise, into Cloud for Customer (C4C), and eventually Sales Cloud V1. V2 is SAP's answer to all of that history: a microservices architecture, zero-downtime quarterly releases, and a clean OData V4 API layer that fully replaces the older OData V2 foundation V1 was built on.

That history matters because it changes how you should plan. If your organization is running V1 or C4C today, V2 needs to be treated as a brand-new project rather than a migration path. PDI (Partner Development Infrastructure) extensions don't transfer over, every integration has to be rebuilt against the new endpoints, and any custom UI work needs to be redone inside the modern web component shell.

⚠ Migration reality check: Treat V2 as a V1 upgrade and you'll blow your timeline and your budget. V1 extensions built on PDI simply don't carry forward — plan from the outset for a net-new implementation, not a lift-and-shift.

A Sales Process Built Around the Rep, Not the System

Before diving into individual apps, it's worth understanding how V2 reshapes the day-to-day sales workflow itself.

The Digital Sales Workplace: A Command Center for Reps

At the center of Sales Cloud V2 sits the Digital Sales Workplace — essentially a daily command center purpose-built for sales reps. It pulls together everything a salesperson touches in a day, from prepping for calls to tracking how deals are progressing, all in one place.

The main screen combines a prioritized to-do list, an integrated calendar, and one-click access to the tools reps rely on most: call lists, tasks, opportunities, leads, emails, and the sales navigator.

That consolidation translates into speed. Reps can create accounts, contacts, tasks, appointments, leads, opportunities, and quotes — or log a call — without ever leaving the dashboard. A Kanban-style pipeline view rounds it out, letting reps drag and drop deals across stages so pipeline progress is always visible at a glance.

A Seamless Path From Lead to Quote

Consistency is what makes a sales process scalable, and V2 bakes that consistency in through a connected chain of applications that carry a deal from first contact to close. The standard flow runs through four stages:

Lead → Opportunity → Quote → Sales Order

1. Lead capture

Everything starts in the Leads app, where reps view and manage every incoming lead. Each record carries the details that matter — source (website form, event, referral), contact information, engagement history, lead score, and follow-up status — so reps can quickly judge lead quality and act: schedule a call, hand it off to a teammate, or convert it straight into an opportunity.

2. Opportunity management

Once qualified, a lead becomes an opportunity and moves into the Opportunities app, where a Kanban-style board tracks its progress across pipeline stages. From here, reps can:

  • Drag opportunities from one stage to the next (say, from "Qualification" to "Proposal")
  • Schedule follow-ups
  • Send emails without leaving the workspace
  • Book meetings or site visits
  • Log call notes and meeting summaries

3. Quote creation

When an opportunity matures, the Quotes app takes over. Here, users generate customer-specific proposals, adjust pricing and discounts, apply terms and conditions, and track the deal through its approval workflow.

4. Sales order generation

Once a quote is approved, native integration with SAP S/4HANA (or other back-end systems) converts it directly into a Sales Order — no manual re-entry required.

Turning Data Into Decisions: Analytics in V2

Good decisions start with visibility, and analytics sits at the core of how Sales Cloud V2 helps teams stay ahead of performance rather than reacting to it.

Pipeline Manager (Bubble Chart View) gives the pipeline a visual, intuitive shape: every deal appears as a bubble, sized by potential value and colored by urgency, so reps and managers can spot the opportunities that deserve attention first.

Forecast Tracker brings real-time and predictive forecasting into a single view. From here, sales managers can set and adjust forecast targets, compare actual performance against what was forecasted, and monitor trends over time.

Beyond what's built in, Predefined and Custom Reports extend the picture — standard reporting covers leads, opportunities, and sales performance, while integration with SAP Analytics Cloud opens the door to deeper, custom dashboards tailored to specific business needs.

Where AI Actually Shows Up in the Workflow

Sales Cloud V2 doesn't bolt AI on as an afterthought — it's woven through the tools reps and managers use every day.

Lead and opportunity scoring ranks each lead and opportunity using past engagement, deal history, and predictive signals, so it's immediately clear which ones are most likely to close and deserve priority in the pipeline.

Email draft recommender writes personalized email drafts in seconds. Reps can dial in the tone — formal or friendly — and the length, sending faster without starting each message from a blank page.

AI-driven recommendations step in when the next move isn't obvious. The system suggests concrete next steps — schedule a follow-up call, send a quote, offer a discount — based on what's worked in similar deals before, and it even flags upsell opportunities based on past buying behavior.

Account synopsis distills a customer account into a short, clear summary: recent activity, past purchases, open challenges, and overall sales potential, all in a single snapshot.

Predictive analytics takes reporting a step further. Tools like the Pipeline Bubble Chart and Forecast Tracker use AI to project deal outcomes, flagging high-potential and at-risk opportunities and checking real-time results against forecasts — so teams can act early rather than after the fact.

Joule, SAP's AI agent, ties it all together as a conversational assistant. Ask it something like "Which opportunities are at risk this quarter?" or "What's the forecast for Q3?" and it answers in real time, grounded in your own company's data.

The Big Q1 2026 Story: Joule Studio Goes GA

If there's one headline from this release cycle, it's this: Joule Studio reached General Availability in Q1 2026 — the single biggest development in Sales Cloud V2 since it launched. It's the shift SAP has been signaling since Sapphire 2025: moving Joule from a reactive AI copilot into a proactive agent-building platform.

What Actually Changed

Before Joule Studio, Joule behaved like a copilot — you asked, it answered. Joule Studio changes that relationship entirely by letting you build custom AI agents that run multi-step workflows on their own. These agents connect Sales Cloud V2, S/4HANA, third-party systems, and external data sources into a single automated flow, with no extra tooling and no manual handoffs in between.

💡What that looks like in practice: An agent keeps watch over all open Q2 opportunities. If a deal hasn't had customer contact in 14 days and is set to close this quarter, the agent automatically drafts a follow-up email, logs the activity, and alerts the account manager — no one had to ask it to

To go deeper on building and configuring these agents:

  • Joule Studio — Build AI Agents, Apps, and Workflows
  • What is Joule Studio? — SAP Help Portal
  • Joule Setup for SAP Sales Cloud Version 2

The recommended path: start with core Joule capabilities, configure onboarding and permissions, and only then move on to designing custom agents and workflows around your own business processes.

Sales Intelligence: Scoring Deals on What Actually Happens

Q1 2026 also marked the first real leap forward in deal intelligence. Rather than relying on AI guesswork, the system now watches engagement patterns at the deal level and surfaces risk signals grounded in historical deal-slippage data — patterns pulled from real outcomes, not assumptions.

A few resources worth bookmarking on this:

Opportunity Scoring (Deal Intelligence) — SAP Help Portal. Covers overview screens showing the opportunity score, the factors influencing it, and the underlying scoring concepts. Read it here

View Deal Scoring Predictions — SAP Help Portal. The best source for screenshots of the opportunity score UI, the score popup, the insights panel, the score trend, and the risk explanation view

 

 

SAP Sales Cloud V2 — Deal Intelligence Overview. A broader product walkthrough covering opportunity scoring and pipeline acceleration. Read it here

Guided selling ties this all together, giving reps account insights and timelines, relationship intelligence, and prescriptive next-best-action recommendations — while reinforcing good selling habits through embedded gamification

Pipeline Manager Visualization shows exactly how Deal Intelligence surfaces inside pipeline charts and opportunity views, blending the bubble chart with score visualization.

Analytics, Rebuilt From the Ground Up

Q1 2026's analytics update went beyond dashboards. Redesigned forecasting views now support multi-level drill-down, and V2's analytics layer introduces intent-based navigation — the ability to jump from an embedded SAC story straight to the exact CRM record you need to review or update, in just two clicks.

The December 2025 release laid the groundwork for this: it added currency conversion for amount-based attributes inside embedded SAC stories, along with a live connection to Enterprise SAC that keeps data in the source system and discards it once the story closes — a meaningful step forward for data security.

Release History: What's Landed So Far

Q1 2026 — Joule Studio GA + Deal Intelligence + Forecasting Rebuild

The custom AI agent builder goes generally available, joined by activity-based risk scoring, multi-level forecast drill-down, and improvements to mobile and CTI.

December 2025 — Enterprise SAC Live Connection + Currency Conversion

A real-time live connection to Enterprise SAP Analytics Cloud, plus currency conversion support for global reporting inside embedded SAC stories.

2508 — WalkMe Integration + Migration & Adoption Improvements

This release focused squarely on adoption, onboarding, and transition support across both Sales and Service Cloud. Highlights included:

  • WalkMe integration for guided in-app user journeys
  • Smart content flows for feature discovery and onboarding
  • An improved Readiness Check and better Data Transfer tooling
  • Stronger support for V1 → V2 transition planning

2508 — Opportunity Management & Guided Selling Enhancements

Sales execution kept evolving with sharper opportunity management and guidance experiences:

  • Enhancements to Opportunity Management
  • Improved Guided Selling scenarios
  • Better opportunity execution support
  • A more productive seller workspace experience

2508 — Service Operations & Integration Improvements

Service teams saw updates aimed at operational efficiency and more connected experiences:

  • Ticket management enhancements
  • Integration improvements across service processes
  • Faster navigation and execution
  • Improved visibility into service operations

Q1 2026 — Joule Studio GA + Deal Intelligence + Forecasting Rebuild

Custom AI agent capabilities went generally available alongside deeper built-in intelligence:

  • Build and manage custom AI agents with Joule Studio
  • Activity-based opportunity risk scoring
  • Forecast drill-down with improved analytics
  • Mobile and CTI enhancements

December 2025 — Enterprise SAC Live Connection

Analytics became more scalable across global organizations:

  • Live connection to Enterprise SAP Analytics Cloud
  • Reduced duplication of analytical data

More secure reporting experiences

Q3 2025 — Business Information Extraction Extended to Cases

AI capabilities expanded into service execution:

  • Automatic extraction of business entities from customer interactions
  • Smarter case categorization
  • ML-generated interaction summaries
  • Resolution insights paired with sentiment awareness

September 2025 — Intent-Based Navigation in Analytics

Analytics moved from pure reporting toward action-oriented workflows:

  • Direct navigation from analytics to CRM business objects
  • Faster movement from insight to execution
  • Reduced context switching
  • Improved operational productivity

Where This Is All Heading

Across SAP Customer Experience, the innovation roadmap keeps circling back to three themes:

  • Sales → Intelligent Revenue Execution
  • Service → AI-Assisted Resolution
  • ESM → Connected Enterprise Service Delivery

For more release detail and screenshots, see the SAP Sales and Service Cloud 2508 Release Briefing.

The takeaway: SAP Sales Cloud V2 isn't just an incremental release cycle — it's a platform that's actively becoming more autonomous, more predictive, and more integrated with every quarter. For teams starting fresh, V2 is the clear starting point. For teams still on V1 or C4C, the smartest move is to plan the move as what it really is: a new implementation, not an upgrade.

entry.content:
We are pushing data from graphical view from datasphere to SFTP through replication flow and it is initial load

when we run replication flow a folder is created on SFTP path with Target table name from Replication flow, inside which Initial folder is created and file is generated with a system generated name.

We have 2 questions 
1) can we avoid creation of "Initial" Folder and place final file directly under target table folder 
2) can we give static name to final file which is placed (currently it is a system generated file name)

entry.title: How to Follow an HTTP Redirect in SAP CPI
entry.content:
The HTTP adapter does not follow redirects. Here is how to handle a 302 — and a trap that gives you a 403 on pre-signed URLs.

Many APIs answer a request with 302 Moved Temporarily and a Location header, instead of returning the data directly. The SAP CPI HTTP adapter does not follow that redirect automatically. This post shows how to handle it, and one trap that costs a lot of time when the redirect points to an AWS pre-signed URL.

TL;DR — CPI does not follow redirects. Read the Location header and call it again. But if that URL is a pre-signed URL (AWS S3/CloudFront, Azure SAS, ...), the HTTP adapter re-encodes it and you get a 403. The safe fix is to make that second call from a Groovy script with java.net.URL, which sends the URL exactly as it is.

Step 1: handle the redirect

The CPI HTTP adapter has no “follow redirects” option. By default it even throws an exception on a 3xx response. The standard way to handle it:

  1. On the first HTTP call, set Throw Exception on Failure = OFF.
  2. Copy the Location response header into a property (for example TargetLocation).
  3. Make a second HTTP call to that URL.

For most APIs this is enough. The problems start when the Location is a pre-signed URL.

The trap: a 403 on the second call

Here is a real example. The Basware API (public API behaviour, nothing customer-specific) returns the invoice attachment as a redirect:

GET /v1/accountingDocuments/{id}/attachments/{attachmentId}
-> 302, Location: https://api.us.basware.com/cfsecure/.../INVOICE%20%28copy%202%29%202026.pdf
   ?Expires=...&Signature=W-4m2LqUesX~Bxpzq6CfZ~Upf87...&Key-Pair-Id=...

The second call to that pre-signed URL fails with 403 Forbidden. But the same URL works in Postman or Bruno with “follow redirects” on. Why?

Turn on the trace and compare the Location you received with the URL CPI really sent (RealDestinationUrl). They are different:

Path:    received  ...INVOICE%20%28copy%202%29%202026.pdf
         CPI sent   ...INVOICE%20(copy%202)%202026.pdf      (%28/%29 decoded to "(" ")")
Query:   received  ...W-4m2LqUesX~Bxpzq6CfZ~Upf87...
         CPI sent   ...W-4m2LqUesX%7EBxpzq6CfZ%7EUpf87...   (every "~" encoded to %7E)

A pre-signed URL is signed over the exact bytes of the URL. If one character changes its encoding, the signature no longer matches, and you get a 403. Postman sends the URL exactly as it is. CPI changes it.

Note: this is not an AWS problem. It happens with any URL where the exact encoding must stay the same — AWS S3/CloudFront, Azure SAS tokens, Google Cloud signed URLs, and others.

Why CPI changes the URL

The answer is not in the SAP Help Portal — it is in the Apache Camel source code, which the HTTP adapter is built on. Two layers change the URL:

  • Camel UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder. Camel keeps a list ("BitSet") of characters it treats as unsafe and encodes them. The tilde is on that list (unsafeCharactersHttp.set('~') in the source), so every ~ becomes %7E.
  • Apache HttpClient. It normalizes the URI following RFC 3986. Parentheses are allowed in a path, so HttpClient decodes %28/%29 back to ( ).

Neither layer can be turned off in the adapter. If you want to check it yourself:

  • UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/core/camel-util/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/util/UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder.java
  • HttpProducer: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/components/camel-http/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/component/http/HttpProducer.java

Reading the Camel source is a good habit — it answers questions the docs leave open.

What does not fix it

  • CamelHttpUri / CamelHttpQuery headers. They only change which URL goes into the adapter. The adapter still runs the same encoder. (I confirmed this with a trace: still 403.)
  • Adapter settings. There is no “raw URL” or “disable encoding” option.
  • The %25 escaping trick (SAP KBA 3131448, which I also described in an earlier post, "SAP Cloud Integration — URL escaping hack"). It helps with characters the adapter decodes (the parentheses): send %2528, and it stays %28. But it cannot help with the tilde, because the adapter always encodes ~ to %7E and there is no input that keeps a plain ~. So a configuration-only fix is not possible here.

The fix: call the URL from Groovy

Since no setting stops the encoding, make the second call outside the adapter — from a Groovy script with java.net.URL. new URL(str) sends the URL exactly as it is, like a browser or Postman.

Replace the second HTTP call (the Request-Reply step and its receiver channel) with one Groovy script step that reads the pre-signed URL from the property and downloads the file:

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message
import java.net.HttpURLConnection
import java.net.URL

/**
 * Downloads a file from a pre-signed URL captured from the 302 "Location" header
 * (stored in property TargetLocation).
 *
 * Why Groovy and not the HTTP adapter:
 * The CPI HTTP adapter (Apache Camel + HttpClient) always re-encodes the URL - it
 * turns '~' into '%7E' and decodes '%28'/'%29' to '(' ')'. A pre-signed URL is
 * signed over the exact bytes, so any change -> 403. java.net.URL sends the URL
 * exactly as it is.
 */
def Message processData(Message message) {

    def messageLog = messageLogFactory.getMessageLog(message)
    def rawUrl = message.getProperty("TargetLocation")?.toString()

    // Guard: nothing to download
    if (rawUrl == null || rawUrl.trim().isEmpty()) {
        if (messageLog != null) {
            messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Termination_Reason", "Missing_TargetLocation")
        }
        throw new IllegalStateException("TargetLocation is empty - no URL to download")
    }

    HttpURLConnection conn = null
    try {
        URL url = new URL(rawUrl)                    // keeps the URL byte-for-byte
        conn = (HttpURLConnection) url.openConnection()
        conn.setRequestMethod("GET")
        conn.setInstanceFollowRedirects(true)
        conn.setConnectTimeout(60000)
        conn.setReadTimeout(60000)
        // Pre-signed URL is self-authenticating -> no Authorization header.

        int rc = conn.getResponseCode()
        if (messageLog != null) {
            messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Attachment_HTTP_Status", String.valueOf(rc))
        }

        if (rc >= 200 && rc < 300) {
            // Read the body in 8 KB chunks. Fine for normal documents;
            // for very large files, use a streaming pattern to avoid OOM.
            def buffer = new ByteArrayOutputStream()
            conn.getInputStream().withStream { input ->
                byte[] chunk = new byte[8192]
                int n
                while ((n = input.read(chunk)) != -1) {
                    buffer.write(chunk, 0, n)
                }
            }
            byte[] data = buffer.toByteArray()

            message.setBody(data)
            def ct = conn.getContentType()
            if (ct != null) { message.setHeader("Content-Type", ct) }
            message.setHeader("CamelHttpResponseCode", rc)

            if (messageLog != null) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Attachment_Size_Bytes", String.valueOf(data.length))
            }
        } else {
            def err = ""
            try {
                def es = conn.getErrorStream()
                if (es != null) { es.withStream { err = it.getText("UTF-8") } }
            } catch (ignored) { }
            if (messageLog != null) {
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Termination_Reason", "Download_Failed")
                messageLog.addCustomHeaderProperty("Error_Detail", "HTTP " + rc + ": " + err)
            }
            throw new RuntimeException("Download failed, HTTP " + rc + ": " + err)
        }
    } finally {
        if (conn != null) { conn.disconnect() }
    }

    return message
}

A note on using Groovy for HTTP

SAP recommends the HTTP adapter by default, and that is a good rule. But for a public pre-signed URL, the usual reasons against Groovy do not apply: no Cloud Connector, no client certificate, no proxy needed on Cloud Foundry. Two things still matter, and the script handles both: add MPL log entries so the call is visible in monitoring, and be careful with memory for very large files (stream instead of loading the full body).

Summary

  • The CPI HTTP adapter does not follow redirects — read the Location header and call again.
  • A 403 on a pre-signed URL is usually URL re-encoding, not authentication, and it is not AWS-specific.
  • The adapter re-encodes the URL in two layers (~ → %7E and %28 → (), and this cannot be turned off. When unsure, read the Camel source.
  • The %25 trick (KBA 3131448) fixes the parentheses but not the tilde.
  • To send the URL exactly as it is, use a Groovy script with java.net, and add MPL logs.

References

  • SAP KBA 3131448 — HTTP Receiver adapter discards encoding of special characters in address
  • SAP Cloud Integration — URL escaping hack (my earlier post): https://medium.com/@vbalko/sap-cloud-integration-url-escaping-hack-34a45f5a1f37
  • Apache Camel UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/core/camel-util/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/util/UnsafeUriCharactersEncoder.java
  • Apache Camel HttpProducer: https://github.com/apache/camel/blob/main/components/camel-http/src/main/java/org/apache/camel/component/http/HttpProducer.java

entry.title: Subject: Company Code tab/data not visible when adding BP role FLVN00 to existing Business Partner
entry.content:
Hi experts,

I'm working on a Business Partner setup in S/4HANA and running into an issue when assigning the FLVN00 (CVI: FI Vendor) role to an existing BP.

 

 

 

Situation:
- I have an existing BP that is already linked/connected (BP-Customer/Vendor relationship exists in some cases).
- When I try to change the BP role to FLVN00 for a BP that doesn't yet have Vendor data, the system does not show any field/section to enter Company Code data (no way to link/create the vendor company code segment).
- On BPs where FLVN00 already existed beforehand, the Company Code tab appears normally with existing data (Company Code, Reconciliation Account, etc.), so the configuration for existing roles seems fine.
- The issue only happens when adding the FLVN00 role as "New" to a BP that doesn't have it yet — the Company Code data entry screen/tab simply doesn't appear.

What I've checked so far:
- BP Role Category FLVN00 configuration (Define BP Roles) looks standard.
- BP View / Interface Control settings appear correctly mapped to FLVN00.

Questions:
1. Could this be related to Field Groupings (Configure field attributes per BP Role) hiding the Company Code fields for new role assignments specifically?
2. Could it be a Number Range / Account Group assignment issue preventing the Company Code segment from being generated for a "new" vendor role?
3. Are there specific SAP Notes relevant to this "new role, no company code tab" scenario (as opposed to the more commonly discussed "tab missing entirely" issue)?

Any guidance on where to check next would be greatly appreciated. Thank you!

entry.title: Federating, Replicating, and Caching Remote Data in CAP — Part 1
entry.content:
Last year and this year at reCAP I had the honor and opportunity to give two talks on CAP performance optimization with data replication and caching. While I was able to introduce the core ideas, 20-minute talks are way too short to discuss everything that matters in practice. So, here are some more details I want to share:
Almost every CAP application on BTP sooner or later needs data that lives somewhere else. An S/4HANA system, a SuccessFactors instance, another CAP service, some REST API. And every team ends up answering the same questions: Do we fetch it live? Do we copy it? Do we cache it? And in which project have we used the required handler code we need to copy over? 😄

What I'll present here didn't emerge in a vacuum. It's been inspired by many great resources on the topic: SAP's Service Integration guide, the xtravels reference sample (which consumes xflights), the Risk Management ext-service branch, Kai Niklas's CAP Remote Services walkthrough, and Gregor Wolf's cap-replication-demo.

This two-part series is my attempt to share the full picture:

  • Part 1 (this post) covers the theory: the terminology, what CAP provides out of the box, where the gaps are, and how a combined set of open-source plugins closes them with small code examples along the way.
  • Part 2 is a live walkthrough: we set up a small demo application (available on GitHub) that federates, replicates, and caches remote data, step by step.

Terminology

Before diving in, it's important to straighten out the terms as they overlap with terms the CAP team uses in the Service Integration guide. The vocabulary below is what the rest of this series uses.

Federation

Federation is the umbrella term and here means: integrating data from various sources into your CAP application's data model and exposing it as a unified interface. Regardless of where the data physically comes from and regardless of whether queries run live against the remote or against a local copy. This follows the broader industry meaning ("make multiple data sources appear as one"). Please note that CAP's own Data Federation guide uses the term more narrowly, as it there specifically means copying remote data into the local database so it can be joined with local data in SQL. In this series, that narrower meaning is called replication, and federation is the umbrella above all strategies.

Delegation

Delegation is a strategy within federation: incoming requests are live-forwarded (delegated) to the remote service at request time. The CAP application does not persist the remote data in its local model, it acts as a transparent proxy, translating each query on the way out and each response on the way back. This includes OData-specific mechanics like $expand and navigation, where local data and remote data need to be combined at runtime into a single response for the client.
Delegation shines when data must be fresh (think value helps, stock levels, prices) and hurts when the remote is slow, rate-limited or gets hit once per row.

Replication

Replication means the data is physically copied from the remote source into the local database and integrated directly into the application's data model. The copy runs as a separate, scheduled process, (mostly) decoupled from incoming requests, so the application can serve the data as if it were local. Well... it actually is. Full SQL, joins with your own entities, analytics, no network hop per request are the benefits. The trade-off is the flip side of delegation: data is as fresh as the last sync and someone has to run that sync reliably. You potentially need scheduling, delta detection, retries, monitoring and more.

Caching

Caching is deliberately not a third strategy. It's an option on top of either one: a delegated entity can cache responses to avoid hammering the remote with identical queries and even a replicated entity can cache expensive local aggregations. Keeping caching orthogonal avoids a classic design mistake,  a cache that quietly becomes an unmanaged replica.

To summarize:

TermWhat happens to the dataWhen queries hit the remote
FederationUmbrella: remote data becomes part of your app's modeldepends on strategy
Delegationstays remote, proxied liveevery request (unless cached)
Replicationcopied into the local DB on a schedulenever at request time
Cachingoption on either strategyon cache miss

What CAP provides out of the box and what's missing

CAP's support for consuming remote services is genuinely good and the Service Integration guide ('Calesi' — CAP-level Service Interfaces) has been updated a lot over the last year and is a must to read. It explains all the required building blocks:

You import the external service definition and CAP mocks it locally out of the box. You declare a consumption view as a plain CDS projection on the remote entity:

using { API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as remote } from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';

entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner   as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName as name
};

A client's $filter=name eq 'ACME' is translated to BusinessPartnerFullName eq 'ACME' on the remote. Renames, $select restriction, all handled by CAP out of the box. 

Replication is similarly approachable at first: add local persistence to the projection (@CDS.persistence.table), then page through the remote and upsert what it returns:

async function syncCustomers() {
    const remote = await cds.connect.to('API_BUSINESS_PARTNER')
    let page, offset = 0
    do {
        page = await remote.run(SELECT.from('A_BusinessPartner').limit(1000, offset))
        await UPSERT.into('ReplicatedCustomers').entries(page.map(mapToLocal))
        offset += 1000
    } while (page.length === 1000)
}

cds.spawn({ every: 600000 }, syncCustomers) // sync every 10 minutes

This is roughly the pattern SAP's own xtravels sample implements in srv/data-federation.js, and what Gregor Wolf's cap-replication-demo shows end-to-end against the S/4HANA Business Partner API.

So what's the problem? Well... the problem is everything around those lines:

For delegation:

  • Cross-service $expand. The added one-liner to delegate incoming requests works until a client expands from a local entity into the remote one (or the other way around). Now you're splitting the query, running both halves, and stitching results back together, for every affected entity and in both directions. The Service Integration guide shows how; it's real code, per entity.
  • Navigation and filters across the boundary. $filter=customer/name eq 'ACME' on a local entity with an association to remote data needs the same manual splitting and some more advanced custom handler coding.
  • Writes. Forwarding CUD requests means translating payloads through the renames too and deciding, per entity and operation, whether writes should be allowed at all.
  • Resilience. The remote will be slow or down at some point. Without timeouts, retries, and ideally caching, its problems become your problems, live and per request.

For replication:

  • Scheduling. Something has to trigger the sync. cds.spawn, a job scheduler, a heartbeat endpoint. And while CAP provides the tooling, per project someone has to build this.
  • Delta detection. Full loads don't scale. Tracking a high-watermark (modifiedAt, a key, an OData V2 date/time-field pair) is state you now own.
  • Retry with backoff. A flaky network at 3 a.m. shouldn't leave you with a half-synced table. And a 401 should not be retried the same way as a 503.
  • Concurrency. Two app instances, one schedule. Who wins? You need a guard or you get double syncs.
  • Idempotency. UPSERT, never SELECT-then-INSERT, or replays create duplicates.
  • Observability. When did the last sync run? How many records? Did it fail? Without a tracker and some management UI/API, the answer is "check the logs."

And the biggest one: it's not a generic approach

  • Nothing of what you build here is reusable. The delegate handler, the sync loop, the scheduler, the retry logic. All of it is written per entity and per project. The next federated entity means another handler,  the next project means the same patterns and the same coding all over again.

None of this is a criticism of CAP itself as it provides all the tools to stitch together your own integration logic. And that's a deliberate design choice. Data federation is varied and highly use-case dependent. A full-blown federation framework inside the CAP core would not be a good idea. But between "all the primitives are there" and "assemble them yourself, every time" sits a gap for something that removes the repetition without giving up the flexibility. Well... if you keep reading, I might have good news for you. 😄

Connecting the dots with a combined set of plugins

This is where a set of open-source plugins comes in.

 

  • cds-data-federation
    The annotation layer: @federation.delegate and @federation.replicate on consumption views
  • cds-data-pipeline
    The engine underneath replication: scheduled, traceable READ → MAP → WRITE jobs with retry, delta tracking, and a management API
  • cds-caching
    Optional response caching on either strategy

The core principle that everything follows from:

The consumption view is the federation contract. The CDS projection declares the schema and which fields, what shape, what renames. The @federation.* annotation declares the runtime behavior strategy, what to delegate or replicate, optional cache, opt-in writes.

You already model consumption views today as per official documentation, they are the initial projection. The plugin just reads what's there and adds logic based on your chosen federation strategy.

Delegation, declaratively

When an entity is annotated as @federation.delegate, the plugin will forward all incoming requests to the remote service. Before, it infers the source service and entity, the projected columns (which become the $select upper bound on every remote call as fields you don't project are never fetched) and the bidirectional rename mapping. 

using { API_BUSINESS_PARTNER as remote } from './external/API_BUSINESS_PARTNER';

@federation.delegate
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner          as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName  as name,
        Industry                 as industry
};

On top of what CAP's query translation already does, the plugin also handles the parts you'd otherwise code by hand: cross-service $expand in both directions (local → remote and remote → local, including cross-provider cases), navigation filters across the boundary, and static where clauses from the projection.

Caching on top

As mentioned before, caching is not a third strategy as it's an option on the annotation, and it comes in two flavors, that are relevant in delegation scenarios:

The response cache targets the "same query, hundreds of times" pattern: responses are cached by their query signature via cds-caching, with TTL and tag-based invalidation. Only identical queries (sharing the same CQN query) hit the cache; everything else still goes to the remote.

@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'response', ttl: 30000 } }
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner { ... };

The entity cache goes further: instead of caching individual responses, the plugin pulls a full snapshot of the remote entity into a local SQLite table living right beside your application. That's the same pattern the replication cache from the CAP-JS-Community common plugin uses.
Internally for the snapshot pull, cds-data-pipeline is used under the hood (more on that engine in a moment). Any query against the annotated entity, including filters, sorting, aggregations is served from the entity cache... until the TTL expires. Only one remote read per TTL window is sent to the remote service with arbitrary local queries in between:

 

@federation.delegate: { cache: { strategy: 'entity', ttl: 3600000 } }
entity Customers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner { ... };

Replication, declaratively with a pipeline engine underneath

The second strategy is physically moving the data from the remote service into the local database. This can be done via the exact same projection, but a slightly different annotation:

@federation.replicate: { schedule: 600000, mode: 'delta', preload: true }
entity ReplicatedCustomers as projection on remote.A_BusinessPartner {
    key BusinessPartner          as ID,
        BusinessPartnerFullName  as name,
        Industry                 as industry
};

This is where the second plugin enters the picture. cds-data-federation doesn't implement syncing itself, it binds every @federation.replicate entity to a pipeline on cds-data-pipeline, the engine package. A pipeline a simple (potentially scheduled) READ → MAP → WRITE job between exactly one source and one target CAP service. The engine plugin also contributes precisely the list from the "missing" section above:

  • Scheduling (schedule, plus preload for an initial load at startup)
  • Delta detection with configurable high-watermark strategies (timestamp, key, OData V2 date/time fields)
  • Retry with exponential backoff — and a sane default policy: 4xx errors are not retried, everything else is
  • Concurrency guard via optimistic locking, so two app instances never run the same sync twice
  • Idempotent writes via UPSERT
  • Streaming — large datasets are paged and streamed in batches, never held fully in memory
  • A tracker and management OData service at /pipeline: every pipeline, every run, record counts, durations, errors — plus actions to trigger or flush a sync manually

While up until now everything just was annotations, replication also has support for more complex scenarios. If you need to hook into a sync to enrich records mid-flight, the engine emits CAP-style events for each phase that you can hook into to apply your custom logic. 

 

const pipelines = await cds.connect.to('data-pipeline')
pipelines.before('PIPELINE.MAP', 'ReplicatedCustomers', ({ records }) => {
    for (const r of records) r.industry ??= 'UNKNOWN'
})

And because the engine is an independent package, it's also usable without any annotation. CallingaddPipeline({ source, target, schedule }) registers a sync programmatically, for cases that aren't remote-service consumption views at all. For this series, though, think of it as the machinery that makes @federation.replicate production-grade.

Finally, the plugin also provides a UI and OData service to manage the pipelines, start and observe runs and get an overview of the replication "landscape" within your application.

Choosing a strategy

There is no golden rule for data federation, as the strategy is highly depending on various factors. My rule of thumb after having built both ways many times:

  • Delegate when freshness beats latency: value helps, transactional lookups, data that changes constantly, low request volume.
  • Delegate + cache when the same queries repeat: dashboards, dropdowns, anything read-heavy where 30 seconds of staleness is fine.
  • Replicate when you need SQL: joins with local data, aggregations, analytics, high request volume or a remote service you don't want in your request path at all.

Since the strategy is just an annotation on the consumption view, switching between them later is a one-line change. The schema contract stays identical. 

When this is not the right tooling

For completeness: this is application-layer integration and the CAP app owns the contract. If your joins should disappear into the database, HANA synonyms or Smart Data Access are the better layer. If you're building an enterprise-wide data fabric, that's SAP Business Data Cloud / Datasphere territory. And if the integration itself (transformations, B2B, routing) is the deliverable, that's Integration Suite. The comparison matrix in the docs walks through the alternatives row by row.

What's next

That's the theory. In Part 2 we get hands-on: a small demo application (source will be on GitHub) that consumes a remote CAP service, where we build up delegation, caching, and replication step by step, including the pipeline monitor and... since everything interesting needs to have AI these days, we will also also integrate CAP's newly introduced mcp annotation and talk to our federated API directly from Claude..

Regarding the plugins: Any feedback, questions and war stories from your own integration projects are very welcome. Again, data federation is multi-faceted. This is my idea how to tackle this on the back of the mighty features CAP provides. If you have an idea what's missing, how to make things even more flexible, please reach out to me on social media and let's discuss.  

And lastly, if you like the ideas and want to learn more, there is a dedicated documentation portal to read more about all the features from the various plugins... and even more links you can take a look into:


Documentation portalhttps://mikezaschka.github.io/cds-data/
cds-data-federation on npmhttps://www.npmjs.com/package/cds-data-federation
cds-data-pipeline on npmhttps://www.npmjs.com/package/cds-data-pipeline
cds-cachinghttps://github.com/mikezaschka/cds-caching
Source repohttps://github.com/mikezaschka/cds-data
CAP Service Integration guidehttps://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/integration/calesi
CAP Data Federation guidehttps://cap.cloud.sap/docs/guides/integration/data-federation

entry.title: ERROR AU 133 ASSET
entry.content:
Standard Transaction F-43:
1. POSITION) MOVEMENT 100 ON ASSET 130
2. POSITION) SUPPLIER

RECORDING ATTACHED.
ERROR AU133 IS TRIGGERED ON AREA 50.
THE ASSET DOES NOT HAVE AREA 50.

AREA 50 IS DISABLED FOR THE ASSET CLASS.

WAITING AN URGENT RESOLUTION.
THANK YOU.

BR

entry.title: Re: The New Explore Migration Objects App, The Game Changer in Planning Your Data Migration
entry.content:
@Halser_T 

Hello Tobias, 

Thx for providing your nice ideas. Several years ago, we already wanted to provide an Data Migration Planning app where you could store such data. The idea was to provide a project plan (Gantt-Diagram) with tasks for each object of your project scope. The tasked per object and project were based on the SEAMAP structure. You could track the status and also enter things like person responsible and so on including integration into SAP Cloud ALM. Customers we should the prototype wanted to have it. But it was like always in bigger companies, if you don't find a sponsor to support your development idea, there will be no development in the end. 

But the ideas are still interesting, and maybe there will be a new sponsor for this now...

By the way the new enhanced version of the EMO app will show which scope items are active in your system and which migration objects are available.  You can check out my blog about the new features:

What’s New in the Explore Migration Objects App: R... - SAP Community

BR

Frank

@Martina_H, @vishnuvardhantanguturu,

entry.title: Can a SAP Community page stay on the page?
entry.content:
Hello,

It is very annoying when I open 3 links to a SAP Community post in other tabs and I go to the 2nd link, after spend 10 minutes reading the first one, it reloads to the land page of SAP Community without the option to got back to the page I intend to read.

Thank you

entry.title: Re: MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents
entry.content:
Great post, especially the section on MCP being a connectivity protocol, not a semantic layer. Yes!

The write-operation risk you mention is the one I take seriously: for reads, bad tool design costs tokens, for writes, it costs real business documents.

I'd love to see one more ingredient on top of gateway governance: a propose-then-approve step, where the agent suggests the transaction and a human or even a good ol' deterministic rule releases it before anything posts.

Will the upcoming deep dives cover recommended patterns for write tools? Would be grand!

entry.title: Reading the Values of the Data Fields of Comconent Consumption Widget from POD 2.0 in SAP DM

Following data fields are configured for a batch managed component.

Now when I tried to read the respective values of the aforesaid data fields dynamically from the UI (component consumption form) & pass the same as the inputs to the PPD, I can't find any suitable way to do that.

In the PPD the data fields are defined as the structured array.

1.  Now if I use the "insert data" option to read the values dynamically from the UI like below, there are no such inventory ID or batch_no fields available.

Kindly help me to find a solution here.

Note: - All the data are created locally.

With Best Regards,

Puspita Dutta

entry.title: Re: Issue with File Corruption When Downloading from DMS...
entry.content:
@khouloud_1 OK, I was able to get my DMS and BTP working again, I was able to upload a PDF and then download it to a different location and all was fine.

Now, let's here about your case:

  • Are you doing anything special to your document -- encryption, versioning, virus scan or anything else?
  • Does your download also not work in test mode?
  • What type of document?
  • What error do you get indicating it is corrupted?

entry.title: AI Skills Library, CodeConnect, MCP Gateway, CDS-Caching 2.0 | SAP Developer News
entry.content:
Description

SAP AI Skills Library

  • https://skills.cloud.sap
  • Blog post: https://community.sap.com/t5/artificial-intelligence-blogs-posts/the-ai-skills-library-is-live-discover-share-and-build-ai-skills-together/ba-p/14442276
  • Tutorial: https://developers.sap.com/tutorials/ai-skills-library.html

Live from CodeConnect

  • Website: https://code-connect.dev/
  • UI5Con: https://openui5.org/ui5con/
  • reCAP: https://recap-conf.dev/
  • HANA Tech Con: https://hanatech.community/

API-centric Integration and MCP Gateway

  • API-Centric Integration in SAP Integration Suite: A new paradigm for API & Integration practitioner: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-in-sap-integration-suite-a-new-paradigm-for-api-amp/ba-p/14438245
  • MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite: Your APIs, Ready for the Age of Agents: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/mcp-gateway-in-sap-integration-suite-your-apis-ready-for-the-age-of-agents/ba-p/14438250
  • API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 1: Build and Deploy Your API: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-on-sap-integration-suite-part-1-build-and-deploy/ba-p/14438357
  • API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 2: API Governance with Developer Hub: https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/api-centric-integration-on-sap-integration-suite-part-2-api-governance-with/ba-p/14438473

CDS-Caching 2.0

  • Announcement from Mike Zaschka https://www.linkedin.com/posts/mike-zaschka-7395949_sap-cap-cloudapplicationprogrammingmodel-share-7479651652631961600-kfa0/
  • GitHub: mikezaschka/cds-caching: Plugin for SAP CAP providing a powerful and flexible caching service

CHAPTER TITLES

00:00 Intro

00:10 SAP AI Skills Library

01:58 Live from CodeConnect

03:47 API-centric Integration and MCP Gateway

04:50 CDS-Caching 2.0

Transcription

00;00;01;00 - 00;00;06;09
Announcer
This is to develop a news for July 16th, 2026.

00;00;06;12 - 00;00;09;20
Nora von Thenen
Okay. I want to share something with you today that I think is really cool.

00;00;09;26 - 00;00;27;00
Nora von Thenen
It is called the AI Skills Library and you can find it under Skills<dot>Cloud<dot>SAP. And the reason why I think it's cool is because that is a collection of a lot of skills for an AI agent, right, that you can reuse from other people, but that you can also, for example, check out and learn from other people.

00;00;27;00 - 00;00;47;20
Nora von Thenen
But you don't only find skills, you can also, for example, find MCP servers and they're compatible with Joule Work and with Claude. So you can just import them into your system. And let's just have a look at how that works quickly. So yeah here for example you can find a skill for the CF CLI. So the Cloud Foundry CLI and there's the description for the skill.

00;00;47;20 - 00;01;08;17
Nora von Thenen
This is also how the agent that knows which skill to use when. And then here we can just import it to our Joule Work Desktop. And now it's actually importing the skill to my Joule Work okay. So we will update that. Great. And now you can see I have the CLI skill I had before the Joule A to agent skill and Joule CLI skill.

00;01;08;17 - 00;01;33;14
Nora von Thenen
I had those three imported before already and now I also have the CF CLI skill. Now you might wonder what is inside this skill. So you can check out the markdown file here. And it's really just a very very detailed description of how the CLI works, what the agent can use, which commands. For example, there's also a YAML file example somewhere here for the manifest YAML.

00;01;33;14 - 00;01;52;14
Nora von Thenen
And it's really just really detailed description of how to use the Cloud Foundry CLI, which is honestly helpful for humans and also very helpful for your agent. Whatever you're using to then just support you with using the Cloud Foundry CLI, for example. But there are so many different skills out there, and I really, really want to encourage you to create your own skills.

00;01;52;14 - 00;01;58;04
Nora von Thenen
Share them up here. And yeah, just let's contribute and reuse skills from one another.

00;01;58;06 - 00;02;17;20
Nicolai Schoenteich
Hi everyone. Welcome to the SAP Developer News. Were here at UI5Con in St. Leon/ROT and we had a fantastic day of sessions coding, connecting, talking to people. And we can't wait to come back next year. And we can't wait for the next events coming up as part of Code Connect tomorrow or the day after.

00;02;17;22 - 00;02;41;18
DJ Adams
So hello from reCAP 2026. It's Wednesday, the 15th of July. reCAP is well underway. It's been an awesome conference so far. Keynote from Daniel Hutzel, which really showed how central to AI for us and for you CAP really is. Anyway, we've had some great sessions. Looking forward to a nice evening event as well. See you soon.

00;02;41;21 - 00;03;00;07
Witalij Rudnicki
talking about AI, the concept of agents is not new. Right. Even though this book says artificial intelligence and modern approach, and this is the book where the agents were described for the very first time in the context of AI, how many of you know this book and know from which year it is.

00;03;00;09 - 00;03;02;15
Audience Member
74 ish?

00;03;02;17 - 00;03;11;14
Witalij Rudnicki
Now it's actually 1995 book. But yeah, from today's perspective, 74/95 sounds equally ancient.

00;03;11;17 - 00;03;16;21
Nora von Thenen
And our competitor SAP is also an alphabetical them as well.

00;03;16;22 - 00;03;46;26
Nora von Thenen
So that's pretty cool because then you can use all the interference to build your agents using the elements from their hand. And that's what I did today. And I was like a lightning. And then I obviously added a tool. I will show you the code for that in the second. And that tool is calling me. And then once you do this, agent two using the protocol, because then our information discovered by other agents and then we will call them agent.

00;03;46;29 - 00;03;50;06
Nora von Thenen
Cool.

00;03;50;09 - 00;04;20;22
Antonio Maradiaga
Holla, SAP developers. In case you missed it, the MCP gateway in SAP Integration Suite is now generally available. API centric integration is here. It is a new paradigm to how we create our API artifacts and MCP servers on the new Integration cell runtime. Think of it as designing the API and its mediation logic in one place. With the MCP gateway, we can turn our existing APIs into tools for AI agents with authentication, rate limiting, and traceability.

00;04;20;24 - 00;04;50;03
Antonio Maradiaga
Check out blog posts to learn more about API centric integration and the NCP gateway. Also, Ashutosh published a couple of blog posts in SAP community that works you end to end on how to build and deploy your first API on integration self, and then how to govern it through the Developer Hub. All of this available in the BTP trial, so you can go and check it out and get some hands on experience.

00;04;50;05 - 00;04;53;19
Antonio Maradiaga
Hasta luego.

00;04;53;22 - 00;05;19;10
Thomas Jung
And speaking of reCAP and all things CAP related. I wanted to point you towards the CDS-Caching project by Mike Zaschka. It's in its 2.0 version. He's just updated it for CAP 10. I can tell you I've already used it in a CAP 10 project. Found it very handy. I'm using it to catch some remote data that's frequently read.

00;05;19;12 - 00;05;44;28
Thomas Jung
Easy to set up, easy to use. Like so many of the CAP plugins. It's got settings for both memory based caching which would which is what I was using originally, but I had a multi server instance. So then all I had to do is flip a configuration and it used on a database based caching. So super easy to adjust with the configuration settings.

00;05;44;29 - 00;05;59;28
Thomas Jung
If you have a use case and many of the new features, as Mike points out, like the MCP server can really benefit for some caching. If you have such use cases, then definitely check out this great open source plug in to the CAP framework.
